===== 20241115_How_To_Find_Winning_Products_For_Dropshipping_FREE_Product_Research_Method [ssV0Jms9Xxs] =====

by the end of this video you're going to know step by step how to find winning products for your Drop Shipping Store completely for free you don't need to spend hundreds of dollars on expensive paid tools you don't need to spend hours of your time researching all you need to do is follow the simple five-step process that I have outlined in this video to find your next winning product before we get started you have to actually know what a winning product looks like because how can you look for something that you don't even know what it looks like yourself so there's really six criteria that I look for when I'm trying to find a winning product and it has to check each one of these boxes that I'm about to tell you number one is the product has to solve a problem I know there's a lot of people out there that will just sell any random Gadget or little cute toy that they find as a little Drop Shipping product I'm not a huge fan of that I like selling real products that solve real problems the second piece of criteria is a product has to have good margins so for example if I find a product on AliExpress I want to sell myself and let's say it's $15 on AliExpress I have to sell that product for a minimum of $45 in order for the margins to make sense because I can tell you firsthand from experience if you don't have margin in mind when you're actually researching products you're going to actually sell a product that's successful and then realize that you can't make any profit off of it because you're selling it for such a low price don't make that mistake you have to check this box before you start selling the product the third piece of criteria is the product has to be lightweight I don't want to ship products like refrigerators and car parts and like this table it's just going to cost me a fortune and shipping cost and eat into my margins even more I want to ship something that I can fit basically in a shoe box in an Ideal World something that size or smaller is the perfect product for you to sell number four the product has to be Evergreen this is a way to say that the product sells year round this isn't a product that has Seasons if you sell a product for example that is a beach toy there's a very clear season when people are going to be buying that product and it's not in the winter it only peaks in the summer so I want to have a product that is active and that has a active Market 365 days out of the year no dips no fluctuations there's already so many fluctuations you have to deal with anyways when you're running paid I don't want to add another variable on top of that the fifth piece of criteria that I personally look for is the product has to be validated and what I mean by this is the product that I want to sell has to be selling for a competitor at the volume of at least $3 to $500,000 per month if they're selling anything less than that monthly volume that is not a product that I personally want to sell myself I'm not a fan of finding products that aren't currently selling right now and trying to sell those that's not what I do that's not my philosophy it never has been I like finding products that are already selling for people and then simply different differentiating myself because again you want to keep the variables as low as possible so if you find a product that's already proven to sell you can remove another variable out of the equation of your success the sixth piece of criteria is the product has to have the potential for good upsells are there products that are similar to the one that I'm selling that I can also sell and have the opportunity to upsell my existing customers for additional products so if I was selling a pair of glasses specifically the blue light blocking glasses and somebody buys that product the perfect cross sell opportunity there is to sell the the case with the glasses I always want to begin with the end in mind I don't want to find a product that sells first and then try to work upsells around it I want to know from day one what I'm going to be upselling and cross- selling to people so that I can Envision the business being successful before I even start it now let's get into the five steps to find your first winning product so step number one is to pick a niche I always love to say you should always sell what's called hard outcomes and this typically comes down to most people just wanting three things they want more money more sex and they want to lose weight most of human desire can be boiled down into those three things so ideally the product that you are selling is getting people one of those three things some nichas that I recommend that you just can't go wrong because they're really red oceans there's just so many so many hungry buyers in these markets it would be nichas like skin care pain relief Hair Care basically anything within health and beauty is a phenomenal Market to be in the pet Niche is a great Niche and the last one I would recommend and this would be like very much so at the bottom but you can sell kitchen related products I would that would be the last thing I would sell so start from the top down typ people are just more responsive to pain in general you notice the first few ones that I listed off there are associated with pain it could be like back pain neck pain any type of physical pain that people have but it also could be like emotional pain that's why skincare is like a multi-billion dollar industry every single year that's why hair care is also a multi-billion dollar industry every single year and if you pick a niche like skincare think about it that already crosses off a bunch of the criteria that I told you earlier it's already lightweight it's already Evergreen it's definitely validated hey guys Mark here in this video I'm going to show you how to make high converting ads for your brand and drop shipping business that have made me over $542,000 the biggest mistake that I see people make is actually twofold the first biggest mistake that they make is they try to reinvent the wheel right and this is one of the worst things that you can do because if you're just starting out you want to minimize the variables that you have that are keeping you from making money and one of those variables is trying to just make ads that you think are going to convert don't think okay you want to look at what is already working and simply add your own unique twist to it right take the concepts that are already working for other people and then make them work for yourself and the second mistake that I see people make that just drives me crazy is they try to rip content meaning they'll finding ads and Performing and they'll literally steal it one for one now this is a terrible terrible strategy because dating all the way back to when Facebook ads starts if you rip somebody's content Facebook will be able to tell they save something on your ad called metadata and metadata is specific data associated with the image or video ad that you're running so Facebook sees that you stole somebody's ad all they'll do is shut down your ad account reject the ad or worse just completely Hey guys, Mark here. Um, fine [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] left me, but I still made $11,000 yesterday. So, in this video, I'm going to show you exactly how I did it with branded drop shipping. Now, you know, drop shipping is nothing new. It's been around for a while. And because of that, everybody typically is telling you the same few things. And this is what I like to call pump and dump drop shipping, also known as churn and burn drop shipping. Now, there's really three main characteristics of pump and dump drop shipping. Number one is you sell this untapped winning product. Like there's all this dialect in the drop shipping space. It's like, "Oh no, man. You have to get in on a product early. So before it takes off, like you can make all this money. You want to sell products that nobody else is selling." That's kind of the first characteristic, which number one is [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] stupid. Number two is you have people that rip content. And so what I mean by rip content is when you actually find, you know, that untapped product, which again, stupid, and you decide to run some paid traffic to it, you steal content off the internet. You steal even ads like clip by clip, word for word. You maybe change one thing just because you think the metadata is going to be different. And the third characteristic, honestly the most important of all is you lose money. And then the best part is that you just repeat this cycle. Like this is what most people do. Then they fail, lose money, and then they go back and repeat the cycle over and over and over again. I have a very different strategy from this. It's basically the opposite of those three characteristics that I gave you. Okay? So, instead of selling untapped product, I actually sell what I call validated products, but because I want to stay with the lingo, you know, we'll call it a saturated product. I like to use the word validated because product saturation doesn't actually exist. What does exist is angle saturation. So, a marketing angle is a specific way that you're selling a product. Perfect example, everybody buys a product for a different reason. Like, not everybody buys one single product for the same reason. Think about why you bought an iPhone. If I ask 10 different people, I could get 10 completely different answers. And it's the same thing with the products that you're selling on the internet in your drop shipping business. Not every person is going to be buying for the same reason. So, I like to find products that are already proven to sell. There's already so many variables that are going on when you are running a new business, starting a new business from scratch, which is what drop shipping is. It's not like a side hustle. It's a real business. And when you do that, what happens is there's so many variables. You have the creative variable, the funnel variable, you have the product itself. Is this something actually people want? Why would you want to add more variables to your plate? Find a product that's already proven to sell. And second, we don't rip content. There's actually only three ingredients that you need. And if you get these three right, then everything else would be really, really easy. But it's not easy to get these three ingredients right unless you watch the rest of this video. So the first thing of course is actually finding the right product. Okay. So when I say saturated product, let's actually define what this actually means cuz it's one thing to say it, but it's another thing to actually know how to go about and find this product. So when I say when I mean saturated is that there's at least one competitor that is selling this product at scale. I want to see at least around $300,000 a month from one competitor. If there's multiple competitors at that scale, guess what? even [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] better. Like so many people see that as a bad thing, but it's not a bad thing. It's actually what I call a purple ocean strategy. I believe I took this from Josh Snow who came up with it first, so shout out to him. But this is when you take the blue market, the blue ocean strategy, which is selling in a completely non-competitive environment, and then the red ocean strategy. You can combine them together so that we're actually selling something that is red. There's a lot of competitors for this product. But the key, the secret is that you sell this product with a completely untapped angle. again because most competitors are typically selling their products on one angle. So you can come in and scoop up all this very very profitable market share and actually scale your business selling on a completely different angle to whatever your competition is selling at. That is the whole philosophy that makes this work. If you try selling the exact same product with the exact same marketing to the exact same audience, what's going to happen? You're going to lose money. Why? Because they have a data advantage over you. What I mean by that is that they have already spent so much money on their ad account that they have a pixel advantage. They have an ad account advantage. They know exactly what works and what doesn't down to the [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] dollar. And you're never going to out compete somebody like that. You have to come in with another competitive advantage, which is competing on angles. And so when you're looking for this product, these validated saturated products, they need to be doing at least $300,000 a month. What I look for is at least 300,000 visits per month with the similar web extension, which I'm going to show you in a second. The other thing that I'm looking for with this particular product is it has to be a problem-solving product in a passionate market. So, I know there's a lot of people out there that sell like fashion products or sell cute little toys or whatever it is, but it doesn't directly really solve a painful problem in a passionate market. So, you want to make sure that you're selling a product that solves a problem in a passionate market. You need to have those two ingredients together because at the end of the day, entrepreneurship is just solving problems. So every product to an extent solves a problem, but they're not all in a very passionate market. So this is something they absolutely want to sell. If I had the choice to choose between a perfect product and a perfect market, I would pick the market every single day. I'd much rather have a market of hungry buyers than the perfect product. And the third and final criteria when it comes to finding that product is the margins. Okay? By margins, all I mean is like the profitability for you to sell this product at scale without your cost of goods, your cost getting too high that when you try to scale it, you actually end up losing money instead of making money. So, the way that I define this is two ways. Number one, you want to have at least a $20 net margin on your product. So, if you find a product, for example, on AliExpress, you can get it shipped for 10 bucks. You want to sell for a minimum of 30. And then the other rule here is 3x your cost of goods sold. So, for example, you could find a product $40 on AliExpress and you sell for 60. You're like, "Cool, I got the $20 margin." Yeah, but that's not three extra cost of goods. If that was the case, it would need to be priced at 120. And so, as long as the competition is selling somewhere around that price, you can know that, okay, this product is good, is validated, and most importantly, the margins are in place. Really, all you need is something that's very simple and you can simply leverage what other people are doing and add your own unique spin on it so that you don't have to reinvent the wheel and start this entire new business with no validation that it's actually going to work. You can actually pre-validate that your business is going to be successful before you ever even start it. And what most people do and what all the gurus are telling you to do is actually the opposite of what you should do. They're telling you that you should go after these, you know, these untapped winning products, these products that have zero validation that they're actually selling. and you're just like hoping and praying to the drop shipping gods that you happen to strike gold and find this product that just magically makes you money and scales itself. Like it's completely untapped. It's perfect. And this is not the strategy that I like to follow. And the reason why is simply because why would you ever reinvent the wheel when you can find businesses that are already just absolutely printing cash with products that are selling? And my whole philosophy is not going the untapped route, but instead finding a product that is what I like to call validated. A lot of gurus in the space, they call this like a saturated product. I like to use the word validated because, you know, this whole saturated thing is just guru term. But the word validated to me, all it means is that I can prove with data that a competitor is selling the product that I personally want to sell and generating at least $300,000 per month in topline revenue. That's really what I'm looking for. That's what I want to see with a validated product. But here's the key, and this is what most people completely [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] up when it comes to being successful in drop shipping, is they try to completely copy their competitor one to one. They think that it's going to be easier if you sell products that that are less expensive. Or even worse, they sell a product and they're not really converting at the rate that they want to and then they end up lowering their prices, right? People think that it's more difficult to sell products that are more expensive, but that's actually not true. I think the sweet spot for average order value and products that you're selling is around the $1 to $400 range. All right? If you're selling anything less than that, if your AOV is anything less than that, you're just not going to make it. And if you don't think that's possible, well, that's why I'm creating this video in the first place. So, what I like to do instead is this profit maxing kind of blueprint where you actually sell products that are relatively expensive. I actually think kind of the more expensive the better. And the main way that you're able to do this is because you're controlling something through your marketing, through your copy, and through your specific funnel that you're running. And what you're going to control is called your POV. This isn't point of view. This is your perception of value. So, what most people don't understand, the noob drop shippers out there, what they don't understand is that you can control the perception of value around your product through your marketing, instead of just selling some cheap thing and like ripping everybody's ads and just trying to make it profitable. It's a race to the bottom. You're just going to lose long term. What I do, on the other hand, is I build funnels that elevate the perception of value that I can sell the same product as you for two to three times the price. All right? And that's what I want to show you guys in this video. Now, you've probably seen this before, but there's really kind of two ways that you can run funnels right now. First, you have what I call the the basic bitchel, where is you have your ad right here, whether that's on Facebook, whether that's on Instagram, Tik Tok, wherever. Then you drive all that traffic directly to a product page, PDP for short, and then they go to the checkout page. They complete their purchase, right? And then they hit the thank you page, and that's kind of it. That's it at that point, right? This is the TYP, thank you page. At this point, the customer's gone. You'll probably never sell that customer again because you have a terrible business. And even worse, your average order value is very, very low. What I'm going to show you guys in this video is how to construct a new type of funnel that I like to call the Chad funnel. Now, I've gone over this many times. I'm actually going to specifically show you how to execute this at a psychological level because that's really the thing that most people miss. It's not that this funnel is just going to like immediately make you a millionaire overnight. That's obviously not going to happen. But what can happen is if you control the perception of value the right way, you can sell more expensive products at the exact same rate, sometimes even at a higher rate when you set up this. So, you're going to have that same ad that you're running, but instead of driving traffic directly to a product page where it's just like sell, sell, sell, immediately sell. Nobody likes being sold to in the first place, right? So, I utilize that concept and I actually send people to a pre-sales page, specifically some type of advvertorial page. All right? If you don't know what an editorial is, it's simply a combination between an ad and an editorial. It looks like an article that some third-party person published completely separate from your company. And so it's almost like a really native UGC ad. Like somebody's just hyping up your product through an emotional story and then converting all that mass desire into the next step, which is going to be the sales page, right? Now, the sales page is very different from the product page, right? The difference here is that the sales page is designed to sell the outcome. The product page is designed to sell the product. So like, if there's one universal thing that you could take away from this video that you're not selling products, you're not selling features, you're not even selling benefits, you're selling outcomes. Everybody buys a product for a specific reason. If the product just disappeared and they still got the outcome that they wanted, they would be happy. All of my marketing is centered around an outcome. What is the ultimate outcome that these people want as a result of using this product? And then from the sales page, you're then going to drive people to the order page. The whole purpose of this page is simply to collect the payment and maximize your average order value. And then I'm going to do a quick pause right here because the whole concept of profit maxing is actually centered around one universal concept. I've ran ads for a long time. I've been running ads since 2018. I spent millions and millions of of dollars on ads in a lot of different niches. And what I've realized is that the universal law of scaling is that ads get more expensive the more you spend, which means your CPA, your cost per acquisition is only going to go up. So even if you're somehow profitable on like the first day, which you know it can happen, it's not likely, but sometimes it does happen. The more that you try to spend, the more difficult it's going to be to scale. So the way to compensate with that is using the profit maxing principle, which is you have to make your average order value so high that it doesn't matter how high your CPA gets, you're still going to be profitable. Okay? And what I mean by so high, like if you want to know if you have a good AOV or not, there's actually a really simple test that you can run. And all the actions that you have to take to align with that idealized version of yourself, you put off until tomorrow. Because discipline is not doing things and getting yourself all hyped up to get [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] done. Like whether or not you want to do something or not is completely irrelevant to you actually getting that [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] done. That's what discipline really is. Cuz the truth of the matter is like I used to be that guy that was like I'll just get it done tomorrow. I used to be the tomorrow guy. All right, perfect example. Let's say I'm filming this video at 11:00 p.m. at night. I have a whole host of other [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] I have to do before I go to bed. My willpower is not high in this moment, but still I'm here in front of you making this video. And if you obey the little [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] in your brain, then guess what? you're just going to be a little [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] in real life. You start with something so small that it almost feels ridiculous for you to do, right? Let's say you want to get in really, really good shape. Okay? And you're slowly going to build on this over time until that habit is so [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] strong that there's nothing that could ever possibly keep you from getting that one thing done. Most people lose because they try to win every single little battle that there is. They don't have a one thing contract. They have like a 10,000 things contract that they have to complete every single day. And when you don't get every single thing done, then it creates this cycle where you're just self-deprecating and not actually becoming the person that you know you want to be. All right? So, what I'm telling you to do is every single night before you go to bed, sign a contract with yourself saying the one thing that you're actually going to [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] get done that is going to move your life in the right direction. Whatever that one thing is for you, whether that's related to business, making money, whether that's related to your your relationships, to your fitness, to your health, just one thing and slowly build on it over time. So, at this point, since I've been doing this for so long, my one thing contract could last me like could be something that takes me 4 hours to do. But I will still every single day get that one thing done. But I didn't start there. I didn't start by trying to climb up this massive mountain because you're never going to stick to it. Maybe you'll do it 1 day, 2, 3 days, but you're never going to do it consistently. So, in this video, I want to actually walk you through the most important metrics that you need to know, like the back of your freaking hand, and when you find one that is outside of KPI for you, exactly how to fix it. So, when it comes to all the metrics within the ads manager and also within the funnel, because if you're not looking at the funnel, aka your store, then you could be diagnosing the wrong thing. For example, maybe your ads are actually performing great. You know, all your numbers are super within KPI, but your funnel is simply just not converting people or it's not converting people at a high enough average order value, which we'll get to that in a second. But the way that I like to explain this is that you have soft metrics and you have hard metrics. So, soft metrics are things that don't involve somebody actually taking an action on buying your product. So a hard metric would be something like an add to cart, initiate checkout, a purchase, multiple purchases, a subscription, etc. But soft metrics, this is all on the ad side. And the most important of these soft metrics is the CPC, right? This is your cost per link click, specifically your link CPC. So when you go to the Facebook ads manager, you'll notice that there's all types of CPC. You want to make sure that you click link CPC. And this is the cost that you're paying Facebook to get somebody to actually click a link on your ad that goes to your website. If you click the regular CPC, technically speaking, if somebody clicks like the expand text section on your ad, that's technically a click. But we don't want to factor that in. So, make sure that you use CPC link. This is the most important soft metric in your entire ad account because this is what you're paying to acquire somebody who is potentially interested in buying your product. Now, the KPI here, if you're running in the US, I say you need to keep your KPI for cost per click around $150. If you're running a big four, I think anywhere from a dollar to $150 is within KPI. But this is one of the first things that I look at because depending on the other metrics that I'm going to get to in a second, this is the indicator. Like this is not the root cause of the problem ever because there's always something underlying the CPC. Maybe it's your creative, maybe it's your targeting. We'll get into that. But this is really like the check engine light and it should prompt you to go into all these other soft metric to identify. Let's say your CPC is outside of KPI. Okay, what is it exactly that's causing that CPC to go up? So next up we have CTR link. This is something that could be influencing your CPC here. Right? Your CTR stands for click-through rate. This is the percentage of people out of the total impressions that Facebook is serving to your audience. What percentage of people are clicking on the link and going to your website? Right? The KPI here is 3%. Doesn't matter which country you're running in. It's most difficult to do in the United States naturally cuz the United States is the most competitive market out there. However, 3% is still the KPI. All right? So, if you go and look at your ads and your CTR is under 3%, that would be something that you need to look into. By the way, guys, I have a few more soft metrics here. The most important thing that I see time and time again that raises your CPC is simply your creatives are not good enough. I know that's like not the answer that anybody wants to hear, but look, it's 2025. Creative is king. Creative is your targeting. Like, all the targeting at the media buying level is like borderline obsolete. We still do a tiny tiny bit, but for the most part, the algorithm has all the power, which means that you as the marketer, the one actually creating these creatives, you have the biggest control over every single soft metric here. And a lot of times, the simple, albeit a little bit annoying answer is that you just have to make [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] better creatives, dude. A lot of times that is simply your answer. Next up on the list, we have CPMs. Probably the trickiest metric out of all these. CPM stands for cost per meal. And what it means is how much money do I have to pay Facebook to get a,000 impressions on my particular ad. So, for example, if I have $30 CPMs, that means I go to Facebook and I say, "Hey, here's my 30 bucks or say, cool. I'm going to take that ad you just made and show it to 1,000 people." All right, that's generally how you want to think about it. I said CPMs are the most complex issue because there's so many things that can impact it. I don't want to unpack CPMs in this video. All you need to know is that if your CPC is outside of KPI, it's above $150. Let's say it's $2, $2.50. The KPI for CPMs, if you're running in the US, should be around $30 to $50. If you're running with extremely high CPMs, that could be influencing your CPC. Now, keep in mind, this should be at the top of your mind. Everything else, if it's outside of KPI, but the CPC is within KPI, you're good. You're chilling. Like, a lot of times I see guys that are running in European countries, for example, like Italy, the CPMs are dirt cheap. You know, they're like €11, €12, €15. And as a result, because it's so cheap to advertise there, it's going to drop your CTR pretty substantially, unless you just have monstrously good creatives, which if you do, shout out to you. You're going to be printing. But ultimately, it doesn't matter that the CTR is technically outside of KPI because your CPC is still going to be so low cuz your cost to advertise, aka your CPMs, is also very low. So that's the general way that you want to think about it. Next up is the hook rate. So we're getting into some custom metrics here. A hook rate is a percentage of people that continue to watch your ad after the first 3 seconds. Your KPI here is 50%. This is only applicable if you're running video ads because this metric doesn't exist if you're not viewing a video. But if you have a hook rate that is less than 50%, this is one thing that I will not compromise on. If my hook rate is less than 50%, like I don't even want to talk about anything else except for creating better hooks for my ads. Another custom metric here that I have is your hold rate. So this is the percentage of people that are watching up to a specific portion of your video. could be 50% video view, 75% video view, whatever, you know, way that you want to set it up. We personally use 50% as that metric. And what you really want to be looking at here is a 25% KPI. So, this of course depends a lot on the length of your ad cuz naturally, if you're running like a 12-minute VSSL, which we do, your hold rate is going to be dog [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] And that's okay. Like, I don't even look at that number in that case. I'm more so looking at some other metrics. But the hook rate is good and the CPC is good, then hold rate becomes mostly irrelevant at that stage. Now the final thing on the soft metrics is your frequency. Now I was debating putting this one on the list. Frequency essentially means how many times is one individual person seeing that same creative. The reason I decided to put it on this list is because in 2025 your creative is your targeting. And a lot of times people will start testing creatives. They're like disguised as retargeting creatives, right? You have the product in the actual let's just say image ad for example and you have this retargeting style ad copy. Facebook picks up on that because once again, your creative is your targeting and they will only serve that ad to people that are already pretty warm, already within your Facebook ecosystem. The problem with that is if you're trying to target cold audiences, top ofunnel audiences, but your frequency is like out of two, something ain't right. Something is broken that you have to fix, probably the creative itself, if not the targeting within the adset level. And that's it for the soft metrics. Pretty straightforward, but these are the first things that I'm looking at. I'm really looking at CPC. And if I see that it's outside of KPI, I want to know why. Is it because the CPMs are super high? And if not, then we'll move over to the other column, which is going to be all your hard metrics cuz maybe you're just a [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] ad god, bro. Congrats. Must be nice. But for us mortals, we got to work on the funnel. So, let's go over to the hard metrics. All right. These are, as I said, the conversion metrics. So, your add to carts, your purchases, your initiate checkouts, people that are taking a direct action that is measurable so that you can actually get people to buy your [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] All right. First up on the list, we have CVR, aka your conversion rate. This is the equivalent of the CPC. This is like one of the main things that I'm looking at when it comes to the profitability of your funnel. Now, important note here, this metric is relative. Like for me the KPI is a minimum of 3% and I think that's a pretty good standard across basically 90% of all the e-commerce brands out there. However, if you're selling a product that is very very expensive and you are running a more complex funnel, for example, pre-sale pages, advtorials, listicles, sales page, order page, upsells, etc., you can actually sacrifice a little bit more because your funnel is doing more heavy lifting and you're just getting really cheap traffic from Facebook. Those all relative, but generally speaking, you want to shoot for a minimum of 3%. Absolute minimum. If you're selling like a $40 product, then I think 4% should be the KPI. And if you're selling something cheaper, then even 5% is realistic. Next up on the hard metrics list, we have the add to cart ratio. So, if you send 100 people to your landing page, let's just say you're running a PDP, right? Standard product page. I want to see at least 10% of those people actually add that item to the cart. So, I want to see a 10% ratio from people who view the content, aka view the landing page to add to cart. If it's anything less than this, this tells me that my sales page sucks, my PDP sucks. All right, I'm getting traffic. Everything on the ad side is good, but people are getting to the landing page and something is breaking because they are clearly not adding this item to the cart. So, you could be getting what's called curiosity clicks, in which case your ads, they may look like they're doing really well. But what about the quality of the clicks that are landing on your landing page? If it's just dog [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] traffic, then of course you're going to see that reflected in your ad to cart ratio, which case you would actually need to go back to the creative, adjust those soft metrics so that you qualify your traffic a little bit more. Next up on the list, we have your add toart to purchase ratio. So, this is like one step deeper down the funnel. For example, if you have like those 10 add to carts that I mentioned previously, I want to see at least three of those go through and actually purchase, which means that is a 30% add to cart to purchase ratio. If I have 10 add to carts, I want to see three go through and purchase. If you have a breakdown here, that means that people are interested like they see the ad, the PDP is good enough so that they add it to cart, but they don't go and complete their purchase. Now, you could add some urgency and some scarcity in there. Yes, of course, we all know those standard persuasion principles. However, what I see a lot of times is that people haven't actually gone through and QCD their own process. Like, have you actually gone through your own website, added it to cart, initiate checkout, and actually purchased from your own business? Cuz if you haven't, you should. You may have something that is broken. Maybe your payment processor wasn't set up properly. Maybe there's an error on the checkout page. Maybe there's something really weird on the add to card page. And being able to divide these metrics this way will help you identify what what that issue is. If there's a massive drop off from add to cart to initiate checkout, you know that there's a problem with the cart page if any. And if there's a drop off from initiate checkout to purchase, then that's a checkout page issue. So notice how I'm basically taking this CVR concept like the conversion rate and I'm trying to divide it into these subcomponent steps where I can actually identify where the breakdown is occurring. I'll tell you guys, I have trained multiple media buyers, multiple copywriters myself, and I teach them all the same framework, which is to ask yourself the question, where is the breakdown occurring? Marketing is just a game of funnels in general. I know I talk a lot about funnels, funnel scaling, chat funnels, whatever, but everything is a funnel. Like at the very very top of the funnel on Facebook on your ads, you have an impression. So somebody sees your brand for the first time. How many people actually will click on that initially? That's the first drop off. That's the first breakdown, right? Typically 97% of people if you're within KPI are never going to click on your ad. So that's like the first drop down. And then you have they click the ad and then they land on a landing page. That's another drop off. And then from landing page add a cart, another drop off. And you just want to like be [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] maniacal about breaking down these big scary metrics like your CPC and your CVR into these subcomponent steps because it's a lot easier to identify the root cause of the issue and you don't end up trying to fix something that wasn't even broken in the first place. So, next up on the list of hard metrics, we have AOV, right? Pretty standard average order value. And the reason I have this list this this on the list here is because this is what I see most entrepreneurs lacking on in general. That basically means that there is zero AOV contribution whatsoever throughout the rest of that funnel. Absolutely nothing. But that's not the only part because what if you want to sell something high ticket? If you want to sell something at like a h 100red bucks, do you want to sell it for 120? [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] no. That's terrible. So the second rule here is actually 3x of your cost of goods sold. Okay, so one of the best examples of this is actually the supplement market because a lot of supplements are typically sourced, of course depending on the supplement, but most of them source right around the $5 to $7 range and they're sold for like $35 to $40. ===== 20250714_7_weird_and_slightly_autistic_habits_that_made_me_rich [SHrWRvcuf9o] =====

Hey guys, Mark here. In this video, I'm going to be going over seven of my weirdest and slightly autistic habits that made me rich. And first up on the list, we have music. And I don't mean listening to music, but playing music. One cool thing about posting content on YouTube when I look like this is everybody makes a lot of judgments about you. They immediately assume a lot of things about you. But the cool thing that a lot of people don't know is I've actually played the piano for over 15 years. And most people when they get into the entrepreneurship space, they're basically like, I need to grind. I need to hustle. and I needed to have no life and no ounce of creativity whatsoever. And I think that's actually one of my biggest advantages that I have continued to exercise that creativity muscle through the creation or playing of music. In fact, I wasn't exactly planning on this, but I am currently traveling in a separate country and I brought this little mini synthesizer here. As you can see, it's super duper cool. I love nerding out on music and just creating stuff. I believe that if more humans spent time creating instead of consuming, you would have a lot better ideas. You'd have better ideas for your business. You'd have better ideas for how to manage your money. You'd have better mental models for looking at the world. You're human. You're not an AI. You're meant to have these new ideas just and like most things, you know, creativity is a muscle. And the more that you use it, the more that you practice it, the better you're going to be able to translate that into your work. I've had some of my best ideas when I'm in this phase of learning how to play like a new instrument, for example. Like this thing has a million different freaking controls on it, man. And I'm by no means an expert, but I just love the process of like tinkering with it and like figuring out how every single individual function works. And when you have that mindset, you can translate that into anything. I'm not saying that you should go out and trying to learn a musical instrument. But what I am saying is that this is the human experience and you're not AI. AI is probably going to take over the world and they're going to be better at just about everything than you, but they're probably not going to be better at creativity. So, you may as well start by building that muscle now and learning how to do something outside of work that allows you to exercise that muscle. Because a lot of times the best ideas will come to you not when you're super super deep in this like hyper concentrated state, but while you're doing something where you're in flow, where you are just having like something speak to you completely unrelated to work. Now, the first law of scale is that you want to have what's called a high AOV to CAC ratio. Now, you may have heard LTV to CAC ratio. It's a pretty standard business assessment, but I like to do AOV to CAC ratio specifically on product launches. And this is what it means. Okay, you have two ingredients here. Number one is your AOV. That is your average order value. basically the average amount of money that somebody will spend buying an order from you. So, for example, if you're selling a $40 product and you have a $10 upsell, your AOV is going to be $50, considering that 100% of people take the upsell, let's just say. And then you have your CAC, which is your cost per acquisition. Specifically, your cost to acquire a new customer. So, if you spend $100 on Facebook ads and you get two customers back in return, you have a CAC of $50. Now, what most people mess up is they have no idea what their numbers actually are, and they definitely don't know what the AOV to CAC ratio is. And what you should really be aiming for here is basically a 2:1 AOV to CAC ratio. For example, let's say you are acquiring customers for $50. You need to have an AOV minimum of $100. So, before you ever even launch your product, you should know with a relative degree of confidence that you can launch this product and have a really solid AOV. Of course, you're not going to know exactly until you acquire data, but this is one of those metrics that you need to know before you actually launch because this is an overall assessment of the health of your business. If you don't know anything about, you know, exiting companies, the LTV to CAC or the AOV to CAC is one of the most important metrics that investors are looking at. It's one of the most important assessments of the marketing health of a business. So, obviously, easier said than done, right? I'll get into some strategies on how to actually maximize this number to 2, 3, sometimes even 5:1, but we'll get there in just a second. The underlying principle here is actually from one of my favorite marketers of all time, Dan Kennedy. And he said that the business that can spend the most to acquire a customer will win. So the product itself is a variable. The pricing, are you using the right pricing? That's a variable. The branding is another variable. The market is a variable. The creative is a variable, the ad copy is a variable, the funnel architecture is a variable, landing page copy, upsell path, the algorithms on the ad platforms. Think about all these variables. And also think about they multiply off each other. So like every additional variable that you add, it's getting exponentially more difficult for you to control any ounce of success, right? It's literally like [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] this. So with that being said, the law is to minimize the variables. For example, sell a product that you can already prove is selling. Simple. Another variable like the pricing. So, if I find a product I can get on AliExpress, for example, for $10, I have to sell that for a minimum of 30. That's like the first rule. The second rule is it has to have at least a $20 margin. Hey guys, Mark here. In this video, I'm going to talk about when I stopped giving an F. That is the moment when I started making the most amount of money. Now, this is a really interesting law because it sounds completely backward, right? How could you possibly be more successful if you're less detached? Well, let me ask you something. If you do a little search on YouTube for the best way to achieve your goals, the best way to be successful, you'll probably find something along the lines of this, right? Where you should read self-help books or you should be visualizing your goals or you should say stuff like, "I'm going to make 10K a month." Then you just repeat that to yourself over and over and over again. But this is literally the worst way that you could possibly actually achieve your goals. Well, probably not the worst, but it's definitely not the best way to achieve your goals. And I'm speaking from experience. There was actually a point in my life where I was perhaps the most disciplined I have ever been when it comes to success. I had read Think and Grow Rich. I was waking up early. I was meditating twice a day. I was reading books. I was obsessing over my goal. That period of time was also a period of time that I made the least amount of money that I've ever made in my entire life. In fact, I was actually losing money cuz I was running my business. But the more that I dove into this self-help development, visualize your goals, all this [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] my business just started tanking. It's actually a really interesting thing to look back on and I didn't understand it for years. I never understood it. And then I started to invest in some of the top mentors when it comes to your mindset in the world. I literally paid one of the top NLP coaches in the world to coach me on my mindset. And it wasn't until I had this conversation with her that I understood why I wasn't successful during this time period. And the main reason why I wasn't successful even though I was doing all the things that I thought you're supposed to be doing comes down to the law of detachment. You see, when you're super super obsessive about your goals, the problem is you are actually creating attachment to your goals. It got to the point where one of my goals was to make a certain amount of money. And so I would sit down every single day, I would meditate, and I would literally visualize myself achieving that goal. like opening my laptop and seeing that amount of money inside of my bank account. And the thought of me not achieving that goal like triggered literal anxiety in me because that's how serious I was about achieving that goal. Now, did I actually achieve it? I [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] didn't. I wasn't even [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] close. And I remember the spirit of my time cuz I was broke as all hell and I just couldn't understand what I was doing wrong. But now I know and I'm here to enlighten you beautiful people. All right. Problem with doing stuff like this, specifically these bottom two, right? self-help books. There's nothing inherently wrong with them, aside from the fact that they all basically say the same thing, but that's a topic for another video. The main problem with you visualizing yourself achieving your goals and repeating yourself, I'm going to do this, I'm going to do that. Doesn't matter if it's a financial goal, physical goal, whatever. Like, I'm going to get in shape. It doesn't matter. What you're essentially doing when you're saying stuff like this is you are reaffirming your existing state of reality. And there's one thing that we know about the law of attraction. Say what you will. I think we can all agree that the universe is a mirror. Okay? The universe is going to mirror back to you what you are yourself, whether that's good or whether that is bad. And if you are constantly saying to yourself, I'm going to make 10K a month. I'm going to be a millionaire. I'm going to be in shape. The real problem with that is, I am going to. What you're really saying is, I'm currently not making 10K a month. I'm currently not a millionaire. I'm currently not in shape. And you're just reaffirming that to the universe over and over and over again. And that is literally entrenching you in the same [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] position that you've been for the majority of your life. As the saying goes, your external reality is a reflection of the thoughts that are going on in your brain, especially your subconscious thought. And if you're constantly reaffirming to yourself that you're not all these things that you want to be, how can you expect to actually be those things? So, what you need to do is completely flip it on its head. Okay? So, I have created what I believe to be one of the most scientifically accurate charts here that has existed in the face of the earth. And this is ultimately what I call the tryhard versus the Chad matrix. Okay, the try hard on the left hand side here is essentially everything that I was doing wrong. I felt like I was doing everything right yet. I wasn't making any [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] money. So, the first thing that I was really doing is I was obsessing over my goals. Like, I would literally write down my annual goals. I would chunk it down into months. I would then chunk that down into weeks and chunk it down to the day. And every single day that I like didn't make that amount of money, it was like self-destructive. It's difficult to go through that when you're constantly reminded how [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] you are. Like that is a tough thing to [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] deal with. Every single day you sit down, you do your reflection on the day and you fail. Day after day after day, I was literally centering my identity around being a [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] failure and not being the person that I know I needed to be. What I should have been doing was basically setting my goal, borderline forgetting about it. All right, think about it. When you place an order on Amazon, you add the product to your cart, you check out. Do you check your Amazon order page every single 5 minutes of the day to make sure that the order is actually going to show up to your doorstep? No, of course not. You literally click the order and then in 2 or 3 days it [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] shows up. There's not a thought in your mind if that order is not going to show up. This is the same way that you should be setting goals. You should set it once, but then you shouldn't be revisiting that goal day after day, hour after hour, week after week. Forget about it. You set the goal, it's entrenched in your brain, and then you focus every other ounce of your energy and of your effort on the inputs around being the type of person that actually has a reality that you want. So if your goal is to make 10K a month, you don't obsess about making 10K a month every single day, every hour of the day, every week, not even every month. There was literally a point in time where I started, you know, actually figuring out how to set my goals where I wouldn't think about that goal for a month, for multiple months on end. Yet, when the time frame actually happened, where I did make that money, I was like, "Oh [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] I did it." And I did it even faster than I was expecting to. But that wouldn't have happened if I was just obsessive about the goal day in and day out. You have to focus all that other energy on actually the process on the inputs, the things that you can actually control. Another thing that tryards do, you focus on visualizing your goals, right? Meditating, you do all the woo woo [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] You shout your affirmations in the mirror. But what you really should be doing is just focusing on developing your craft. Literally just getting the hard skills that you need. Doing the boring thing every single day that actually gets you towards your goals. The most successful people in the world, they do boring ass [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] day in and day out. They're consistent as [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] They're not giving a [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] about their affirmations, about all this other nonsense. Okay? And by the way, it's not affirmations in and of themsel that are the problem. The problem is when you focus the affirmations on the future and you say, "I'm going to be this person. Let's say that your number one goal is just to make $10,000 in a single month through, let's say, branded drop shipping. And not only do you want to make $10,000 a month, but you want to do it as quickly as humanly possible. This is really the secret to XP farming is to achieve your goals faster than anybody else. Now, most people in life, they will never hit a goal like this. And it's not because they're too stupid or they just don't want it bad enough. It's literally just because they don't move fast enough. So, if you wanted to make $10,000 a month through branded drop shipping, let's say it's going to take you a total of 10 products to actually get there, right? Which means you test one, it fails, you move on. You test another, it fails, you move on. But once you get to that 10th product, that'll actually be the one that gets you to $10,000 a month net in profit. What most people do is they take this amount of time that it'll take to test 10 products and they spread it over such a massively long time period. So, if this was a span of let's say like 6 months and this is today, most people would test roughly one product a week or maybe even every two weeks or if you're really really good two products a week, right? The difference between me and you is that I use this process called XP farming where I basically take all of the time and the failures and lessons that it takes to hit that $10,000 month, but I compress the time frame from like 6 months to one month or even to like one week because when you do this, you are stacking skills and you are stacking failures. so much faster than everybody else. Like you're really just competing on failing faster than anybody else. But as a result, you acquire so much experience, so much faster than everybody else that naturally you will be successful faster. So at that point, not only is succeeding virtually a guarantee, but you get to do it so much faster than anybody else. Now a few rules when you're blocking in your deep work. Number one is there has to be a very clear start and stop time. The reason why is due to a principle called Parkinson's law. Parkinson's law basically says that the amount of time that it takes for you to execute a particular task is going to expand to the amount of time that you have set aside to execute it. So if you're like, "All right, I have to like write this VSSL. I have all day to do it." It's going to take all [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] day. And it's going to create like this addiction in your brain where you start to like really look forward to it. And that's exactly what you want. One of my favorite neuroscientists, his name is Dr. Daniel Aean. He had this concept of what he called ants, right? A nt. It stands for automatic negative thought. Because what most people do when they go through something hard like a difficult workout or they take a cold shower is they finish like then they get out of the the ice tub. let's say after like the, you know, an ice bath and like, "Fuck, that was so freaking hard." And like that's their internal dialogue. That is a negative thought. That is an ant, an an automatic negative thought. What you should do instead is say, "Damn, that was hard as [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] but I still [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] did it because that's why I [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] am." Create it. Center it around your identity and turn it into something positive because it's just going to feed and build on itself long term. That's ultimately, guys, like how you build a [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] unbreakable mindset. It's all your selft talk, your internal dialogue. All right, so zooming out here, right? Step one, time blocking. Step two, the reward loop. And then step number three is going to be obviously what you do in the deep work session itself. Like what are you actually spending your time on, right? It's the action that you're actually taking during this deep work session. And luckily for you, this is probably the easiest part because it's actually more important for you to determine what you're going to be working on than it is actually doing the work itself. Because if you start working on the wrong thing, then you're end up you're not going to make any [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] progress. All you need to do is answer these three very simple questions. Question number one is, what is my goal? Question number two, what is the number one constraint that is keeping me from reaching my goal? And then question number three, what is the one system that I can build that will solve that constraint forever? So, let's go back to our e-commerce example. My goal is to make $10,000 a month with branded drop shipping. What is the number one constraint keeping me from reaching my goal? Well, I don't know how to acquire customers on paid traffic without losing money in the process. Right? That's the constraint is my cost per acquisition is too high. So, what is the one system that I can build that will solve that constraint forever? Well, let's just say in this case it was to build highly profitable ads using AI because AI is infinitely scalable. And so this is the simple three-step process of actually executing the XP farming concept. And the cool part is essentially every time that you quote unquote fail, you're actually acquiring XP. you're just like acquiring experience. The point is you're acquiring all this experience, all this failure in a short period of time because you are able to learn faster. That's it. It's just speeding up the process of learning to an incredibly short amount of time so that you progress faster than anybody else. Now, the last thing that I want to say is when it comes to XP farming, obviously, you're not going to be doing deep work all day. Nobody can be doing deep work all day because if you think that you can then you're not actually going that deep in your deep work and that's very telling. So the question is like what do you do outside those hours where you're not doing your deep work. Well that's why I want to show you this. This is the Eisenhower decision matrix. Business is building this thing this entire time without seeing any results and eventually reaching the inflection point where it actually starts becoming fun and you get like this amazing sense of progress and you get that addictive feedback loop cuz progress is addictive. But working hard on something and not seeing anything become of it, that's really, really hard to get through. But there's a simple fix. Like I said, the reason you feel that way is cuz it's external. All you have to do is center that reward mechanism to be something internal. And by internal, all I mean is something that is actually within your control. Perfect example, let's say that you are a salesperson. You're taking high ticket sales calls. You're trying to get to $10,000 per month. You can't directly control somebody paying you money over the phone. However, you can control your reps that you're putting in. For example, one framework that Alex Lormozi has that is phenomenal is he would basically say, I want to collect 10,000 nos. Like, I want to speak to 10,000 people that say no to me because that would be such a massive amount of reps that I would get in that it would be unreasonable for me to not see results. Well, how does that tie in to burnout? Well, the difference is if I center my reward mechanism and my whole identity around how much money I'm making, my emotions are just going to be a freaking roller coaster all the time. It doesn't matter if I'm making $0 a month or $100,000 a month. Money comes and goes in seasons, right? But I don't want my own feeling of self-worth and identity to be centered around it. So, instead, I centered around something that I can control. Let's say in the sales example, doing role plays, right? if I just do 10 times the number of role plays as anybody else as the people in the same business that I'm selling in, I'm just going to blow by them. Not only that, but I'm going to actually feel better. So instead of saying, "Okay, I get this massive dopamine rush when I make money," I train my brain to create this massive dopamine rush when I have a mock roleplay. So if you think of your process in entrepreneurship and making money online, kind of like a spectrum, and this is basically the start, and this is when you're making all the money in the world, and this is where you're making zero. This is the process. Most people, they center their entire reward mechanism around the end result. But the end result, as we know, it's there. It feels good and then it's gone. You need to learn to enjoy the process. Not just for your own sanity before avoiding burnout because burnout only really becomes a problem when you're so obsessed about this and not about this, not about the reps, the process, not about the journey. this amazing study that was conducted for people that were visualizing their goals. And the people that visualized the goal itself were actually less likely to achieve the goal compared to the group that was visualizing the process of them achieving the goal. The simple way to change your reward mechanism from being centered around something outside your control to something inside of your control is a simple two-step process of influencing your subconscious brain. Cuz this is something that's done subconsciously. You know, if I walked up to you and gave you a $100 bill, you'd probably get at least a little spike of dopamine. But we can rewire that using this process. Number one is simply repetition. There's only two ways to influence the subconscious brain. One is simply repeating stuff to yourself over and over and over and over and over again. That's why hypnotists a lot of times when they're speaking to you and they're trying to get into that subconscious brain, they'll repeat, you know, one phrase over and over again to get you into this super influential state. And the second thing is going to be emotion. Because the way that you can create these feelings in your brain is you have to simulate them first in your imagination. So the way that you can do this and the way that I have personally done this is simply by controlling your self-t talk. After you complete something difficult, what you say to yourself, your own internal dialogue is incredibly important in terms of creating that like dopamine rush that you just did something really [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] difficult. You should [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] hype yourself up for it. You should be like, "Fuck yeah, I just did the [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] that I didn't want to do. I [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] did it and I'm [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] proud of myself for that." It's going to sound so incredibly corny. I'm not going to lie. But if you do it over and over and over again, this is how you build your identity around something that's not just installed in you natively from evolution. This is something where you can take control of your own belief system and gify it so that you can achieve your goals faster. So at this point, you're like, Mark, okay, I get it. I need to center my reward mechanism around something that's in my control. Something where it's center around the process and the reps and not the outcome. Now what? Well, the thing about burnout is it's kind of like a big battery. And the battery is going to run out if you do things that drain the battery. But in the same way, it's also going to be charged if you, you know, participate in activities that are going to charge it. Because I think we can all admit we've done something before that was really really hard, but it didn't make us feel burnt out. It actually gave us more energy. And so everything that you do throughout the day, it can be divided into this column of something that is going to charge your overall energy and reduce burnout or take away from it, right? Another thing that is particularly relevant for me is like being on a ton of calls. I'm a super duper introvert and I like my deep work. ===== 20251009_why_your_ads_are_performing_like_sh_t_urgent_andromeda_update [rlsHAWkeS3M] =====

Hey guys, Mark here. In this video, I'm going to show you why your ads have been recently performing like absolute garbage and the Andromeda update from Facebook. That is probably the reason behind it. The Andromeda update is a new algorithm update from Facebook specifically surrounding how Facebook is sorting through these tens of millions of ads and deciding which creatives to show to which individuals. And while this article was written on December 2nd, 2024, this actually wasn't fully rolled out until July. If you had a terrible, terrible July, this is probably part of the reason why. And many people up until right now, it's now October, are still incredibly struggling with this update. And so, Facebook released this article. It's relatively technical. And so, most people say that you read through this article and you basically are more confused than you were in the first place. But I'm going to break it down for you and explain it at a surface level. Because the reason why so many advertisers are struggling right now is because they don't actually know how generally the Facebook machine works in the first place. even before the Andromeda update. If you don't understand that, you're definitely not going to understand Andromeda. So, I'm going to explain that right now. Facebook has a catalog of tens of millions of ads and it has to sift through all of them every single day, millions of times per day. And Facebook is doing all of this, sorting through all these tens of millions of ads and deciding exactly what ads to show you as soon as you open Instagram or Facebook within 200 milliseconds of you opening. Just think about the math for a second. Sifting through tens of millions of ads and identifying three to five that you're going to see within your next few scrolls. That's an insane amount of data. That's why I always say Facebook is a data platform, not an ad platform. So, you have this massive catalog of ads that has to be sorted through to eventually be shown on your customer's phone. And the Andromeda update is specifically a new AI powered brain that is deciding which ads you show to which customer. But this is pretty basic and you probably already knew this, right? So, let's get a little bit deeper. The question is, of course, how how is Facebook sifting through tens of millions of ads, this massive ecosystem of advertising assets, and deciding within 200 milliseconds the exact creative to show to your customer? Well, it's actually a four-step process. Step number one in this process is called retrieval. And this is where Facebook is taking those tens of millions of ads, filtering it down by well over 90% into thousands. Literally just taking millions of ads, finding the ones that are generally relevant to your audience, and turning them into thousands. The next step is called light ranking, which is where you take the thousands and turn them into hundreds. After light ranking, you go into final scoring, which is you take those hundreds of ads and you actually begin to rank them based off what's called total predicted advertiser value. And of course, the fourth step is going to be where the actual auction happens, where you're competing directly against your competitors for the creative that's going to, according to Facebook, resonate most with your audience and eventually be served that impression. Okay? Now, this step alone, I'll let you guys in on a little something. Facebook released this kind of diagram many years ago. The OGs in the advertising space will know what I'm talking about, but they released this diagram that explains actually how they calculate the total advertiser value of one singular creative. So, when you're going directly head-to-head against your competitors, here's what's actually going on behind the scenes. Okay, let's break this down cuz this is the total value formula for who's going to win this last step of the filtration process. Okay, you can see the total value equals the advertiser bid, which if you're using auto bidding, this is going to be done for you. If you're using manual bidding, this is where you can kind of out compete people using your bid, times your estimated action rates. Estimated action rates is going to be a combination of your soft metrics, which will be your CTR, for example, your cost per click, your CPMs, and a combination of that mixed with your hard metric. So, what is your click to conversion rate? As you can see down here, what is the overall quality ranking of your ad? Back in the day, they used to have these old rankings as well that were super helpful. I think they're actually bringing them back, but that's for another video. And then the final piece that they added in here is going to be the overall user experience. This is a piece that they added because if it was only these first two on maximizing advertiser value, you just have all these [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] savage marketers come in that have zero ethical boundaries whatsoever, serve the most [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] black hat ads on planet Earth to these people, and just drive Facebook stock into the ground because the ads are just too out of control. So, they had to add this final piece that's actually optimizing for the user experience. And this is actually going to be reflected in CPMs in the future because if you have poor reviews, for example, on your on your Facebook page and on your ads, you're going to be penalized with high CPMs. Honestly, they've probably been doing that anyways. They're just now being public about it. But that's another story. That is the final step of the filtration process. Back to Andromeda. Andromeda specifically is focusing on one of these four phases of ranking and categorizing ads. And that one phase is also the most intensive phase which is ad retrieval. Can you imagine manually going one by one through tens of millions of ads even if it's done by a computer within 200 milliseconds. I mean it's just insane. And so the reason that Facebook launched Andromeda and specifically focus on the retrieval process of ad filtration is because of AI. Facebook has recognized that there has been a massive surge of AI creative driven material. like people are now just producing so significantly more ads using AI that Facebook had to develop an entirely new system of sorting through them simply because the volume was becoming too much to handle and the algorithm was becoming wildly inefficient. Facebook even admits to this in this article they say one of the reasons they created this because of the increase of advertiser automation with generative AI. They say the sheer volume of ad candidates is three orders of magnitude more than previous stages. Like it's just insane that people are running so many ads written by AI.
