===== 20251029_this_video_will_find_you_when_you_ve_lost_your_purpose_it_s_dark [-aOvW4Ts5DA] =====

Hey guys, Mark here. If you've read the title of this video, you're probably assuming that this is a pretty dark video. But, it's actually not. And I think the longer that you watch this video and the longer that you listen to the words that I'm speaking, the more that you're going to understand that watching this video could completely change the direction of your life moving forward. And I'm not joking when I say that. Typically, when it comes to personal growth, making money online, whatever you want to call it, everybody starts to obsess very quickly around setting goals. They say, "Okay, I'm at point A and want to get to point B as quickly as humanly possible." Whether point B is getting jacked, getting shredded, whether point B is making 10K a month, 100K a month, whether point B is finding your soulmate, your partner, doesn't really matter what it is because we all by default fall into this line of thinking that I'm at point A and I want to get to point B. How do I get there? But, due to, you know, some things that have happened personally over the last few months, I've started to rethink this model of setting goals. And the reason why is because there's going to come a point where you are no longer a physical being existing on this planet. You pass away and very quickly everybody who had some touchpoint with you is going to forget you. They're going to forget you probably a lot faster than you'd ever really want to realize. Most people don't think about this. They don't think about what their funeral is going to look like. They don't think about the people that are going to come to their funeral. They don't think about what impact they're really going to leave on this planet. To me, I think this is silly because this is best place to start. The best place to start is at the end. And the end of the physical world is death. And so, if you are not taking time to figure out what your life actually wants to look like by starting at the end instead of starting at the beginning and trying to set goals and hopefully find your way to some meaningful end, then that may not be the best way for you to live your life. And so, because I was thinking about this a lot, I wanted to apply first principles thinking to this. And so, you're probably familiar with Warren Buffett, one of the greatest value investors of all time. But, Warren Buffett actually had a business partner named Charlie Munger. Also an incredibly intelligent human being this man was. And most specifically, he is known for coining this term called the law of inversion. Let's go back to our example of setting goals. If I am super duper overweight as a vast example, and I want to get into shape, I want to get muscle, I want to get shredded. My goal, of course, is to have a six-pack, lose weight, and look the way that I want to look and feel the way that I want to feel. So, what are the things that I need to do to get there? That's kind of the standard line of thinking. The law of inversion states that instead of asking what you should do to get to your goal, you ask, "What are the things that would ensure that I fail in getting towards this goal?" In the example of me being super duper overweight and wanting to lose weight, what would be the things that I would say that would be perfect evidence that I would fail this goal? Well, number one, I would be extremely undisciplined with my eating. I simply cannot control the foods that I would put in my mouth. That would basically guarantee that I'd never be able to get shredded. What would be another one? Well, I would lack consistency. I would maybe go to the gym for a few weeks, get all motivated, and then I'd get demotivated, not see any progress, not see those physical changes to my body immediately, and I'd quit. I'd also probably have terrible sleep. I would not be prioritizing recovery whatsoever, and I would just be, you know, destroying my body with zero chance of it to actually build itself back up even stronger than it was before. That is what inversion thinking is, and it's pretty easy when it comes to setting goals like, you know, I want to get jacked or want to get in shape or I want to make money. It's pretty clear what you know you should not be doing. The cool thing about the law of inversion is if you want to know what you should be doing, you just identify what you absolutely know with 100% certainty you should not be doing and simply do the opposite. So, instead of being extremely undisciplined with whatever food goes in my mouth, I have to be very disciplined with food that goes into my mouth. Instead of not being consistent and falling off the wagon after two or three weeks in the gym, I have to be obscenely consistent. Even if that means me going to the gym less, period. I'd rather be consistently going three to four times a week compared to going every single day for two weeks and then quitting for another two weeks. And so, as you can see, it gets really clear what you actually should be doing. And I didn't make this video to talk about me losing weight or, you know, me making money in the law of inversion thinking applying to that. But, I made this video instead to apply it to your life. So, if you wanted to live life and have the worst possible existence of all time, an existence where not only did nobody come to your freaking funeral, but maybe they did even like protested it. That would be the worst possible case scenario. If you wanted to identify things that would ensure that you did not live a life well lived, what would those things be? And this is the cool part because the answer is going to be different for everybody. My answers are going to be different than yours. My answer, for example, if I wanted to ensure that I did not live a good life, I would know for a fact that I was generally not disciplined in my life. I would say that generally I would go towards areas of comfort instead of staring directly at the truth even when it's most uncomfortable. I'd be extremely hesitant to put myself in any situation of discomfort because discomfort is scary and has the potential to hurt me, and I don't want to be hurt. And when you go through this exercise yourself, you start to get this list of things that you know you should not be doing. Now, the fun part comes where you get to ask yourself, "Am I doing the opposite of those things?" Because if you notice my list, it had no quantitative goals on that list. Yes, I'm scaling a company right now and I want to exit for a very substantial sum of money. That was not on my list. Even though it's a goal that I set, it was not on my list because ultimately people are not going to remember what you did, they're going to remember how you made them feel. The only thing that's really going to matter at the end of the day at your funeral is the relationships that you created in the process of building that business. That's why I've become quite detached from making a lot of money. Even though I do make a lot of money and I'm going to continue to make more, I know that that's not really what my life is trying to work towards. I'm trying to work towards a life of honor, respect, and discipline. So, I want you to go through this exercise. I want you to go through this law of inversion. And after you do, I want you to set aside some time, 10 to 15 minutes. Go in a room, turn the lights off, no music, nothing. Just sit alone with your thoughts. And I want you to close your eyes and actually visualize your funeral. Who is there? Who is delivering your eulogy? What does your eulogy actually say? And if you don't know, then how can you possibly be living in alignment with the life that you want to live now if you don't know what your funeral should look like? If you don't know what a true life well lived looks like and you don't have that in extreme vivid detail, how could you possibly be living in accordance with that now? If you watch my videos, you're not here to do average things. You're not here to be ordinary. You're not here to be mediocre. And if you're not taking the time to do this inversion exercise and understand in extreme excruciating levels of detail, then you're not going to be acting in alignment with the life that you truly want to live. And that's even if you happen to have crystal clarity on what that life actually looks like. So, that's why this law of inversion is so powerful. And I know this is a little bit of a different video for me, but I hope you guys enjoyed. I'll see you on the next one. Got more ads. Peace.

The difference between a habit and an addiction is how it affects your homeostasis. Let's say that this line here represents kind of your baseline level of being. An addiction is something that you actually begin your day below your baseline and you don't even feel normal. you don't even feel like you're at homeostasis until you actually have that particular addictive substance of choice. And then maybe if you have it again throughout the day, it'll spike up and then you'll kind of slowly approach the baseline once again and actually even go below it back to the addictive baseline. Again, this is the definition of an addiction. Something where you need to have a particular thing for you to feel normal, just for you to be at your baseline. A habit on the other hand is something that elevates your existing baseline. So you actually do begin your day here and when you participate in the habit it brings you up here and then slowly brings you back down to your baseline. So it actually elevates your level of well-being. This would be something like you know exercise or cold exposure or just doing deep work on your business. The first step is going to be the cue. The second step is the habit or ritual itself. And the third is going to be the reward. So, let's take an example. I believe I'm addicted to the gym. Well, let's talk about that. I want to introduce you to the concept. This is something that I actually coined myself, I think, unless somebody came up with it before me. But concept of stoic sparring. I am a huge fan of stoic philosophy. Marcus Aurelius, Epictitus, people like that. I think they give you great frameworks for how to handle the challenges in life, even though they lived thousands of years ago. Stoic sparring is one of the simplest and best ways for you to identify what you should be worrying about and spending time on and what you should not. And all you have to do is get out a piece of paper, make a T-chart like this, and on the left hand side, you're going to write the word outside, and on the right hand, you're going to write the word inside. And what I want you to do is basically do a mental brain dump. All the things going on in your life or in your business onto this sheet of paper. And everything that is genuinely outside of your control, you are going to put over here on the lefth hand side. And everything that is inside your control, you're going to put over here on the right hand side. What you'll notice is that a lot of pain and suffering in humans in your own mind, you're worrying about things that are completely outside of your control. And the stoic sparring exercise is to write down all those things to get them out of your complex brain because as soon as you get it onto your brain and onto paper, you can actually completely let that thing go. So this is literally an exercise that I try to do at least on a weekly basis and often times just get out of my head. So, for example, if there's somebody in your life that is causing you some mental trouble, right? You can fill that in in whatever way you desire, but you got somebody that's taking up mind space in your head. The question is, can you control the way that person is acting? Can you control the way that they're making you feel? And the answer to that, of course, is no. You cannot control what other people are going to do. You can only control yourself and how you are reacting to things. So, what you're going to do is you're going to write down exactly what happened with that particular person and why it's bothering you so much. Take some time to think about it. And then all you're going to do is let that [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] go. I know it sounds very simple, but I actually want you to feel the emotions of that specific thing that you're going through. Like fully feel it. Don't avoid it. Feel that emotion, write it down, and then let it go and never think about it again. Then on the right hand side, you're going to write down all the things that actually are in your control. And what's funny is that when you go through this exercise, what's going to happen is you're going to have all these things on the left hand side of the column. So much [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] that you were worrying about, that you were spending all your precious mental space on that you have zero control over. There's nothing that you could do and yet you're spending energy thinking about them, trying to problem solve them when there's nothing that you can do. And then this side over here is going to be very, very short. And these are actually things of course that are inside of your control that we are going to apply to our daily framework of execution. So, this really helps you do a few different things. Number one, it helps you actually get clarity. A lot of times you think you know what you need to do, but you don't. Now, I want you to look at this title. I included the number 90 days for a very specific reason. 90 days is a very short period of time. So for me, the question becomes, how can I squeeze as much experience and information and learnings as possible into that 90day time frame? And that is how I came up with the concept of XP farming. XP farming is the process of taking what would require most people years and years and years of experience, of failures, of learnings, and shrinking that into a very condensed period of time. What if you could live 5 years worth of experiences in a span of 3 months? How quickly do you think you're going to grow? How much smarter do you think you're going to get? How much more maturity do you think you're going to gain by getting more experience? You see, wisdom is not acquired through time. Wisdom is acquired through experience and XP farming is basically acquiring more you know XP in a very very short period of time. Now fair warning this experience is often times painful often times very difficult and a lot of people will give up because it's so much frequency. It's like you're experiencing 10 times the number of failures as normal. 10 times the number of rejections 10 times [clears throat] the number of experiences of learnings. So, it all depends on how you frame that, of course. But this is supposed to be a relatively intense 90-day period. This isn't like, "All right, thinking grow rich, Mark. I got my belief system. I'm just going to sit back and eat Cheetos for the next 89 days because I watched this video." No, [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] you. Number one. Number two, we want to get better and we are trying to squeeze as much experience and effort into as short a time frame as possible. The issue is most people expand the time that it takes to get a particular project done for no good reason. Okay, this is called Parkinson's law. If you say, "All right, I have this project I got to get done. I'm going set aside four hours." It's going to take four hours. On the contrary, if you have that same project, you say, "I only have one hour. I have to force myself to finish this project in an hour." You come up with more creative ways for me to execute that project at the same level of quality. The big caveat people have here, it's like, "Okay, Mark, if I'm going to do like 10 times the amount of work and effort, I'm going to sacrifice quality." Wrong. That is the one thing that you cannot sacrifice. Which is why I said you have to be more creative with how you are executing these particular projects. For example, I'm the run more ads guy. If I needed to make 10 ads fully edited in a span of 90 minutes, I'm going to have to come up with more creative ways than my traditional process of, you know, scripting with AI, sending it to video editors, etc. I probably have to build some kind of AI workflow to do the editing for me. But it forces you to ask these questions and to build better processes so that you can execute the project faster. This is like you go to the gym and you ask like the biggest dude there. The answer is [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] both. Okay? You have to lift a lot of [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] weight and you got to do a lot of [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] reps. There's like there's no way around this. The same applies to your work. It's going to be painful. It's going to be uncomfortable and maybe even uncomfortable for your bank account. See, this is what most people don't understand about schedules. It's not a game of being psychotic about your tracking and playing out your day. That is addition. Having the most effective day possible is rather a game of subtraction. So on the contrary, let me show you generally what my days look like. This is it. Yes, this is literally it. And one of the ingredients in chief your copy is matching the words that you're using in your copy with the words that your customers are actually speaking in their day-to-day life. This one is very very crucial. Not the most important, but in terms of copyrightiting, it is absolutely up there as one of the most important ingredients, but it's only the chiefing process. It's not like the ideation. That's not like the mechanism, the big idea, all these other ingredients. So, for that reason, we're going to put this one in the B tier. Probably criminally underrated, but nevertheless, we got to we got to put something in the B tier. Next up on the list, we have the purple ocean strategy. Now, you have probably heard of red ocean theory and blue ocean theory. Red ocean, of course, being entering markets with a ton of ton of proven demand, but a shitload of competition, and then blue oceans, of course, being very, very little competition, if any, but also not a lot of validation cuz you don't know if customers are actually buying anything there. I did not coin the term purple ocean strategy. somebody else did, but I like to use it because a purple ocean is going into relatively competitive markets, but carving out your own kind of niche through your strategic angles that you are using. The perfect example is to go into a super hyper saturated market and then focus on a specific demographic. Actually, I'll give you an example right now. Primal Queen, one of the fastest growing DTOC brands out there. Everybody knows them at this point. They were not necessarily early to the whole animal-based organ meat supplement industry. However, they clearly carved out a very, very solid market for themselves by focusing on a specific demographic. Obviously, that demographic being women. You don't have to be first to be great, but you do have to be unique if you're not going to be first. And that's exactly what they did. And that's what the purple ocean strategy is all about. So, for that reason, putting the purple ocean strategy in the B tier. Next up on the list, we have my concept of quantum leaps. Quantum leaps is a concept that I came up with that basically explains that when you're trying to scale your brand, you always start to hit these ceilings. It could be at first at around $2 or $3,000 per day. The next one could be between $15 and $20,000 per day. The next one could be at $100,000 per day. But every business gets to these certain plateaus where what used to work is no longer working now. And the funny thing about these plateaus is that you think you can just kind of scrape your way through that plateau by continuing to test these very, very small things. For example, you just start to test more variations of your top creatives or you start to test more variations of your top landers or you know, maybe you test a new product but using the exact same funnel architecture. My philosophy is that if you want to break through these plateaus and scale faster and more aggressively, more profitably, you have to think way farther outside of the box and take bigger swings with your test. What this means is like testing a completely new funnel architecture that you've ne never tested before. testing a completely new, for example, like ad account structure because completely changing everything in your ad account is a pretty big [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] deal. Another example could be completely altering your creative strategy and testing a new format of an ad that nobody has ever really tested before, at least that you've seen. You have to take bigger swings is the point here. That's why I call them quantum leaps. This isn't just like a stepping stone. This is like the biggest possible leap that you could possibly take. So, for that reason, quantum leaps is going in the A tier. And it's not just because I came up with it. It's just because I find it to be true. Next up on the list, we got Curious George. Hey guys, Mark here. In this video, I'm going to show you exactly how I find winning products for branded drop shipping in 2026. Here's the thing. Most of you watching this video have been brainwashed into believing a lie that the only way for you to actually make money with branded drop shipping is by some miracle, just like digging for gold. Like just one day you get lucky, you find that winning product and boom, you're making six figures a month and you're finally buying that Lamborghini you've had your eyes on for so so long. This is a lie and it is one of the most dangerous lies in e-commerce, period. Because it basically forces you to think in this extremely one-dimensional manner where the only thing that you're actually competing on is the product. And it turns out with drop shipping specifically, that's a terrible thing to compete on because products are commodities, right? You're selling the same product as a bunch of other people. So that's a very, very terrible thing for you to actually compete on and try to make money on. So for most people because they have terrible marketing, terrible creatives, terrible funnels, terrible backend, they only are able to compete on the product and then they actually do fall into this trap of trying to test product after product after product and it starts to feel like you're literally gambling. The reality of finding winning products in 2026 is that winning products are not found, they are created. It is incredibly important that you understand this point because if I get this point across to you, to me, this video is a W. Winning products are not found. They are created. Just think about it. If you're selling the same product as everybody else and you are basically selling it using copy and paste template that everybody else is using. You're not competing on funnels. You're using ripped ads which is the same ads that everybody else is doing. You're not using the same creatives and you're selling the same product in the same market. So like finding the product period. But the second category is what most people miss. And that is the category of product validation. Because I have a philosophy that I don't want to sell anything that I can't already prove is being sold at a high scale. And by high scale, what I mean is at least $300,000 per month in revenue. So many of you are trying to test these products that are not proven to sell in the market in the first place. There's already so many variables for you to make this business successful and get it off the ground, right? You got to worry about creatives, copy, funnel, so many things. And I want to sell something that people are actually already buying. I just want to sell it better than everybody else. And that's why this product validation step is the most crucial step in the entire process. Now luckily for you actually researching and finding these products I have a very very simple list of criteria because again the validation is going to do most of the heavy lifting. My first piece of criteria is I want the product to solve a painful problem in a passionate market. So a lot of people are like oh you want to make sure you sell problemsolving products. I'm like well every product is designed to solve some sort of problem. It just may not be a physical problem. It could be an emotional problem which is why a lot of people buy clothing for example. But I digress. I want to solve a painful problem in a passionate market. And you may be thinking, what does painful actually mean? So the most painful problems are going to be the problems that are the lowest on Maso's hierarchy of needs. For example, trying to sell products related to spirituality, um, you know, ascending your consciousness, self-actualization, difficult to sell, right? Because it's not very tangible. It's not very painful. It's people that are moving towards pleasure and not away from pain. I want to sell to people that are moving away from pain, which is typically going to be one of these three kind of bottom categories here. And the closer you can get to the bottom, the better, cuz that mean it's a more painful problem that you're solving. Now, product research criteria number two is I want to sell a product that has good margins, like high margins. And I have two very simple rules to accomplish this. Number one is you want to make sure that you can sell the product for 3x your cost of goods sold, right? Also called your COGS. Like if somebody's making a lot of money, you know they got good cogs. You know what I'm saying? So that's rule number one. If I'm selling a product for, let's say, $15 shipped from my supplier, I can't sell that product for anything less than $45. Period. That is just baseline bare minimum. You can be a little bit more lenient with this rule. If you're selling something more expensive, like if your cost of goods sold is over $50, then you can be a little bit more lenient, but still, if it were me, I would still be aiming for that 3x cost of goods sold rule. Second rule here is going to be a minimum of $25 of gross profit per order. Now, what does this mean? Let's say example you're getting that product for $15 and you do decide to sell it for $45. So you hit that first rule. Well, you also want to make sure you have $25 worth of profit. Well, if we take 45, subtract 15, that's $30. 30 is greater than 25. Therefore, we meet both rules. The reason I have this one in place is because a lot of people will sell these cheap ads products that are just so difficult to scale in today's competitive environment where your ad costs are going up and up and up where you can get it shipped for like three bucks. Technically, according to the first rule, you could sell for like $9 or $10. And your job is to make everything as easy as possible when you're getting your business off the ground. So, hit those two rules and you'll be in good shape. This is going to be different for everybody, but what you should specifically know is what is your target cost for acquisition to be at your target level of profitability. You do not lose money when you run ads. Period. You do not lose money when you run ads. What does happen is you buy data. And the cool thing about data is that every time you buy data from Facebook or Tik Tok, it's actually telling you a story. It's telling you what to do. And you need to know how to read that data to make decisions so that when you relaunch and adjust your creative, adjust your targeting, adjust your funnel, then you actually do start making money. So for example, if I were to launch a product and I see immediately that the conversion rate is very very low. Specifically, the add to cart ratio is low, which is very very common, right? The add to cart percentage is the percentage of people that land on that first landing page and end up adding the item to the cart. My KPI here is 10%. If I launch and I immediately see this is way off, I know either number one, the messaging in my ads is not getting people warmed up and ready to buy by the time that they reach my lander. Or number two, my lander sucks. It's not actually convincing people to add the item to the cart. It turns out most of the time that you launch stuff and test stuff, it doesn't work good. That's why you need to learn how to read the data and make relaunches. And every single relaunch you do, you should be getting better. Again, you're not losing money. You're buying data. And that leads me to point number three, which is my one relaunch rule. I have a hard rule set in place for most of my students, which is that if you test a product and you buy data and you're not profitable, you should give yourself one more opportunity to turn that losing product into a winner. And this is where you can actually create the winner. Right? So what you do is you launch, doesn't work, you read the data, you adjust, you relaunch. If it still is nowhere near profitable, cut it and move on to the next product. Do I still believe that you can make that product successful? Yes. But the opportunity cost of pursuing that product instead of moving to the next one is too high. So what you can do very simply is launch once, get the data, relaunch, and if your data is not better, just move on. A lot of people try to get super duper creative and they end up testing completely new branding, completely new pricing, new market, new creative, new ad copy, new funnel architecture. Stop. Other times it'll be like the infospace has been doing for years now. It's like if it doesn't work, you don't pay and I'll like Venmo you $1,000. Like Jesus Christ, you can see how far we're going with the evolution of these guarantees. But I think there's actually something for ecom guys to learn from that cuz if you just compete on the amount of time that your guarantee is, you'll go from 30 to like a one-year guarantee to a lifetime guarantee. Where do you go after that? Like lifetime plus the afterlife. like you're guaranteed that you can still get a refund in heaven. No, no, I don't think so. That's not this is not how it works. You got to be more creative with how you're structuring these guarantees. So, next thing you want your landing page to have is going to be a mechanism. You can think of this kind of in two ways like in through the direct response lens. You have kind of the the UMP and the UMP is unique mechanism of the problem and is unique mechanism of the solution. So for example, this is uh some random weight loss product that I pulled up. But if we look at the unique mechanism here, we just have to scroll down a little bit and you can immediately see the unique mechanism of the solution is this fat burn switch, right? Like the reason that you haven't been able to lose weight this entire time. Who would have thought? Just a little biological switch that your body has to switch on and then you'll actually start to lose weight. That is the unique mechanism of the solution because it's the reason why everything that you've tried before that hasn't actually worked. I don't need to over complicate this. All the mechanism is is why is it that this product is actually going to work for me? Like tactically, what is the reason? And why does it justify why everything else that I've tried before this doesn't work? That's all a mechanism truly is. It's like logically explaining why everything else has failed and this is the only thing that's actually going to work for me. Now, number six on the list of things your lander has to have is going to be social proof. This one very self-explanatory. Once again, Primal Queen did a a great job with this. Down here, they have an entire testimonial section, which is always good. Kind of a review section, but they also do something really good that you guys should be doing, too, which is called embedded testimonials. Like don't just throw social proof in here in reviews for the sake of throwing in the reviews and social proof. Like it should serve a purpose. And for me, testimonials should always do one of two things. Number one is it should overcome some sort of objection or number two it should install some sort of empowering belief. You know, in sales they say that if you can handle objections before they actually come up, before they scroll down to the FAQ section, you're going to be converting more people. Period. A lot of times customers have objections. they're just not thinking of them consciously enough to go scroll down to the FAQ. So, if you can overcome those objections with very specific and purposeful embedded testimonials, you can do very, very well. Now, number seven on the list is, you guessed it, going to be those FAQs. Again, the purpose of the FAQs, it is to answer the most frequently asked questions from customers, but more importantly, this is to overcome objections, plain and simple. So, one of the big objections, for example, in the supplement space is what is the quality of these supplements? like, have these been tested by an external third party? Boom, objection overcome. I'm pregnant. Will this work for me? Boom, objection overcome. But again, I think that you can pre-handle these objections earlier without the FAQ section. So, the objection doesn't even become an objection in the first place. It's already handled. ===== 20251126_how_to_test_facebook_ads_post_andromeda_update [z4UAXut0kCA] =====

Hey guys, it's Mark here. In this video, I'm going to show you how to test Facebook Ads post Andromeda update. First, I'll be walking you through my personal Facebook Ads testing structure. Second, I'm going to show you how to actually set up this exact structure within your ad account. And third, I'm going to tell you why this structure works so freaking well. So, the Andromeda update basically flipped my entire creative strategy on its head because pre-Andromeda, I was very heavy on testing a [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] load of variations. So, when you're testing ads, you can kind of put them into two categories. You can put them into a lot more, but generally the way that I think about it is you have your variations and you have your new concept. So, if you can't tell by the name, variations are very small changes to existing winning ads. New concepts are basically completely unique creative material that you're bringing into your ad account. So, like I said, it flipped it on its head. What I used to do was the majority of the ads that I was launching, roughly about like 80% I would say, was actually variations because once you find a winner, you can just create winner after winner after winner after winner and skyrocket your win rate because you're just making these tiny changes to your ads. And of course, new concepts would have been the other 20%. But now, this has basically been completely flipped to the point where my creatives are 80% new concepts and 20% variations. And when I'm testing new ads from like a brand new product, let's say, I am pushing much harder on testing new concepts instead of one concept with a bunch of variations. So first, let's talk about the structure. To launch new ads, I recommend typically using a CBO. CBO stands for campaign budget optimization and sounds like a big fancy word. All it means is that you set the budget at the campaign level instead of ad set level and Facebook will determine how to spend your campaign budget across the various ad set. Now, I'm going to come back to the actual budget and how you want to calculate how much you should spend. But inside of your CBO, you're going to have multiple ad sets. The number ultimately is up to you, but if you're launching something new, I recommend at least two or three ad sets in here. So again, back to a CBO, if I set the budget at a $100, it doesn't mean that it's going to perfectly spend $33 here, $33 here, and $33 here. No, of course not. It could quite literally spend $99 in this one ad set and 50 cents here and 50 cents here. Unlikely to happen, but a lot of times one ad set does cannibalize things, but that's actually okay. This entire philosophy of how I structure my ad testing centers around letting Facebook do the majority of the heavy lifting for you. So, inside each of these ad sets, the ad set level is typically where you set your targeting. So, who are you really going after? And what I do inside of these ad sets is I keep them completely broad, which means Advantage Plus placements. Typically, I'm running in terms of ages 18 to 65 plus and I'm running male and female. Of course, if you're only selling to one demographic, then select the one that's most relevant to you. But if you're selling to both, keep it broad. Now, at this point, you may be wondering, "Mark, why do you have three ad sets if they're all going to be identical? They're all broad, they're all Advantage Plus, and they're all 18-65 plus. Everything is broad. Why do you have three of them?" Well, the reason I have three is because they're not actually identical. You see, inside each of these ad sets, if this is ad set one, this is ad set two and this is ad set three, I'm actually going to have three different creatives in each of these three ad sets. Again, I'm just using three. If you want to have more ads per ad set, you can. But here's the point that I'm trying to make. You want to segment these ad sets by ad concept. So, let's say ad set one is ad concept one, ad set two is ad concept two, and ad set three is ad concept three. And then inside, let's say you have three creatives each, which these three would actually be variations of that one concept, okay? I'll explain kind of what I mean, but this would be a total of, if we count the actual ads, 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8 9 total ads for this ad launch. But these three ads are going to be variations of this one new concept. So, the reason why I said you can have as many ad sets as you want in here is cuz you could have five ad sets in here, but only one ad per ad set and each one is a new concept. I think the sweet spot is not one. I think it's like anywhere from three to 10, but [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] I have ad sets running that have 20 plus ads in there. So, you know, 30 plus ads, 50 plus ads, some of them. There isn't a rule here, okay? I'll tell you what I recommend is anywhere from three to five ad sets, which means three to five concepts, completely unique concepts, and anywhere from two to five variations in each of those ad set. And by the way, this is mostly relevant to images and videos. I typically do my testing with images cuz you can typically just do it faster. But if you want to test with images and videos, you can. I would recommend segmenting them in the ad set or even in a completely different CBO because typically what I see is if you throw a bunch of images and videos in the same ad set, one of them will cannibalize everything else. Like all the videos will get spent and the images will get no spend, which of course you don't want cuz you still want to make sure you test those images, right? So, I'll do a separate CBO if you're going to combine images and videos. For me, I just keep it simple. Just pick one, only videos or only images, and run with that. Now, let's talk about the budget. How do you calculate the budget for a CBO? Well, I have a golden rule that I would have at least $10 allocated out of my total budget per ad in the CBO. So like I said, how many ads do we have in the CBO right now? Well, 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8 9. If we take nine and multiply it by $10, that is a total of $90 a day, a day, yes, that you want to run this CBO ad. So, you take the number of ads across all of the ad sets total, multiply that by 10, and that is going to be the minimum daily budget that you run with. The one exception to this rule is I would never recommend that you run a CBO at less than $50 a day. I don't care what your budget is. If you don't have enough cash set aside to run a $50 a day CBO, you should not be running paid ads. It's my personal opinion. If you don't like it, I do not care. Hey guys, Mark here. In this video, I'm going to be ranking books that will make you dangerous. Now, I have to give a disclaimer for this video. Everything that you read in these books, some of them are pretty dark and very manipulative, very aggressive, and I ask that you only use them for good. You're probably familiar with Robert Greene from The 48 Laws of Power, which is his most notable book, but this is actually a book that I prefer over The 48 Laws of Power because it's not just about power acquisition, but more about the nature of human beings and how we operate. And what I especially like about this book is that it'll explain everything in the form of a story via some historical character. And it really just generally explains how humans, we generally put on some sort of front, some sort of inauthentic facade. And if you dig really, really deep, you can see people have, a lot of times, very poor mal intentions that are often times designed to hurt you, designed to manipulate you. And if you don't know what those are, and you don't know how to spot them, then you're just going to be taken advantage of all the time. That's why it's important for you to know these laws, but not to use them on other people, just so that you know them, so that you're not being taken advantage of yourself. So, for that reason, I love this book. It is a monster. It's like a freaking textbook. I'm going to put this one in the B tier. Next up on the list we have Power vs. Force by Dr. David R. Hawkins. This is, hands down, one of my favorite books of all time. Not even books that'll make you dangerous. Like, this is legitimately just one of my favorite books of all time. And the reason why is, similar to Laws of Human Nature, it explains how there's a layer beneath everything that we know about our way of operating, deeper than our emotions, deeper than our own conscious thoughts, that is our consciousness. And you can see the subtitle of this book, it's called The Hidden Determinants of Human Behavior. Deep down, we all have a level of consciousness that we're operating at. Everything around you has some sort of level of consciousness. And in this book, you can identify and understand what those levels are, how to ascend from one level to the next, and how to even descend from one level to the next, which you obviously don't want to do. I recommend a lot of my students to use this book because they are selling products on the internet, and you have to influence people to sell products on the internet. ===== 20251216_instant_gratification_is_a_cheat_code_actually [QdDY2rYRssU] =====

Hey guys, Mark here. In this video, I'm going to be going over how instant gratification is actually a cheat code. You'll see all across social media people saying that instant gratification is going to be your biggest downfall. It's the worst thing that ever happened to society. And that's only true for the people that allow it to take control over them. There's actually nothing inherently wrong with instant gratification. Instant gratification is not the enemy. What is the enemy is the reward. Let me explain what I mean. The way that I think about instant gratification is any time that you take an action very shortly after you experienced a dopamineeric reward. Dopamine is that lovely neurotransmitter that we all know and love that is associated with pleasure and reward and motivation. And instant gratification is unique in that the time between the action taking place and the reward being triggered in your brain is incredibly short. Most people would associate instant gratification with things like scrolling social media where you get that those micro hits of dopamine or maybe watching porn or eating highly satiating foods. Notice that the time between the action actually being triggered and taking place and the reward being triggered in your brain. This time span is always incredibly fast. If we compare this on the other hand to delayed gratification where you take one action and then what happens after the action? Nothing. Then there's another action. And then of course there is another action. And sometimes this process will repeat over and over and over again for days, weeks, sometimes months or even years on end before you ever really get that reward and that payoff with the dopamine when you get that dope reward as we'll call it. If we compare this to instant gratification, naturally of course the time elapsed is very long. That's hence why it's called delayed gratification. But that's not the point of this video. The point of this video is to say how you can actually use instant gratification to your advantage. As I said in the beginning of the video, the problem is not instant gratification in and of itself. The problem is the reward that you're associating with the action. Let me explain what I mean. See, the way that most of you are using instant gratification is you have the action and of course you do get the payoff in dopamine. However, this dopamineergic reward is something external. This is something outside of your control. Of course, yes, you can control the actions, but you can't control the reward. At least most of you are not using it in that way. So, all you have to do in order to use instant gratification to your advantage is simply swap the dopamineergic reward for something external in exchange for something internal. And there's really three ways to kind of do that. There's three ways for you to use instant gratification to your advantage. So way number one is simply going to be to rewire your reward around the process instead of around the outcome. This is something that I talk about constantly all over my content. The reason that you're so unhappy with your life. It's not that complex. It's simply because you're placing your happiness and your fulfillment and your worth into things that are outside of your control. Perfect example. I talk a lot about making money, especially making money online. If you attach your self-worth, your validation, your security, if you attach anything internal to something that is outside of your control, you're literally just asking for a life of pain, unhappiness, and depression. And that is why for most people, instant gratification is something that they are a slave to instead of vice versa. What's a good example of this? For me, in anything, I always like to use the gym as an example because it's so representative of so many things in life. So when you go into the gym and the action that you're actually taking is of course, let's say, lifting weights. Typically a relatively painful process, but you can rewire your brain to make it something within your control. Think about it this way, especially when you're starting out in the gym, there's really no reward. Like you don't really have enough muscle mass yet to like feel really good. Maybe you'll feel good afterwards, but that's not instant gratification. That takes a lot of time for you to complete the workout. What is the instant gratification that you can control here? Well, well, simply put, it's literally just mentally rewarding yourself through your own personal self-t talk to rewire these pathways in your brain so that every time you do complete a difficult rep, anytime you do something really, really difficult physically in general, instead of having your lower self say, damn, that sucked. Hey guys, Mark here. In this video, I'm going to be going over 10 brutal lessons I learned in 2025 after generating roughly $10 million from branded drop shipping. Lesson number one, listen to your customers, not your competitors. I don't know why it is this way, but for whatever reason in drop shipping, branded drop shipping, it's a bunch of [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] copycats. You got one person copying another who actually copied another person, and that guy actually copied this guy. And what happens is you end up falling into this trap where you actually think that what your competitors are doing because they must be successful, right? It's just like everything that they're doing just must be successful, which is stupid in the first place. But you fall into this trap to thinking that your competitors are basically better than data directly from your customers. And that is simply not true. If you kind of take off of your e-commerce glasses and you just look at business in general, the businesses that end up succeeding at a massive, massive scale, I'm talking about businesses like Facebook, like Google, like massive, massive companies. They're ones, especially early on that obsessed over customer feedback. Now, let me ask you a question. How many of you that have generated at least one customer in e-commerce have actually hauled one of your customers? How many of you have spoken to them on the phone? It's almost [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] nobody. Yet here you go looking on the Facebook ad library, looking on whatever you know tool you want to use to spy on them to figure out what it is that they're doing when you have the best source of data right in front of you staring at you in the eyes, which is the words of your own customer. A lot of times when you fall into this trap of just obsessing over your customers, you end up succumbing to a little something I like to call good old shiny object syndrome and you just chase, you know, this winning ad or this new format or this new funnel type. when in reality, if you just spoke with your customers, you would immediately know what it is you're missing. And even better, if you take that feedback from your customers, what they're saying, and add a little AI on top of it, then you get even more deep insights. But the point here is you don't need to play this obsessive copycat game in order to win, in order to make real money. What you do need to do is understand the fundamentals of selling and marketing, which is not copycatting, by the way. Understand fundamentals and then speak with your customers. Understand how they speak. Understand the emotions that they're actually feeling. Not the emotions from your research, but from the purest form of research that there possibly can be, which is direct words from your customers. Now, this rule, there's actually a logical fallacy that is associated with this. And it's called hasty generalization. I'm telling you, if you want to live a better life, understand and be aware of your own logical fallacies and biases. Hasty generalization is essentially the thought process of it works for them, so it must work for me. When in reality, you have a different business. You may have a different product, a different market, different angle, different website, different funnel, different ads. There's so many variables, but you think works for him, must work for me. Wrong. That is a logical fallacy. And the second kind of logical fallacy here is something called survivorship bias. If you have not read about survivorship bias, this is the best example that I've ever heard because it was true to me. I was going into college. I remember I would look up at these entrepreneurs that I I idolized at the time. People like Bill Gates, like Mark Zuckerberg, and I was like, "These guys had such a successful business that they dropped out of college." It's like kind of a normal thing now, but back then it was like this that was such a [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] you to the system. I loved it. I was like, "I'm going to start business in college. I'm going to drop out. It's going to be the biggest [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] you to the system. I love it." This is the perfect example of survivorship bias. I thought the best entrepreneurs were college dropouts. And if you look at many of the top ones, they are. But that is a bias because if you zoom out on average, still to this day, if you have a college degree, you are better off in the far majority of ways compared to if you don't. Now, I'm not talking about you guys. You're not average. Okay? If you're watching this channel, I already know that based off data. True. And the way that this applies to ecom specifically is what I see a lot of people doing. You know, you test a funnel, it's getting some sales, but it's like not quite profitable. And then you ask the perfect question, which is a good question to ask. Why? Why is it that my customers are not buying at the rate that I wanted to? I want to increase the conversion rate. So, what do you go do? You go to the worst possible [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] place that you possibly could, which is your comment section. It's like Facebook [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] comments where all the lowest of the low people hang out. What you're doing is you're focusing on the exact wrong place to be looking. Why not speak to your customers and ask them why they bought? And even better, a better question to ask is what was something that almost kept you from buying? Basically, you just have to try harder. And after doing that so often for so long, I realized that this is a complete lie that people tell you so that you stay trapped in these states of being super distracted and never getting any real meaningful work done. True focus is simply the absence of distraction. It's not trying to be a maximalist and have a pomodoro timer and all these other tiny little cheat codes, so call them. It's literally just to reduce and eliminate all the [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] that's getting in the way of focus, which sounds relatively easy, but it's actually a little complicated. Hence the reason I'm making this video in the first place. So, what we'll call for this video true focus really has four kind of steps. And if you follow these four steps, you'll get into the deepest possible states, not just once, but every single day repeatably because you literally just have a formula. First is called preloading, which is basically what you do before you sit down. Second is activation that triggers you to get into that state. Third is immersion, which is where you actually maintain that deep state of focus, what people call deep work or deliberate practice. And then fourth is the exit protocol. If you don't close that loop properly, then cool, you got deep work done once. to achieve true focus once, but you can't do it tomorrow or the day after, every single day following. So, let's talk about preloading first. The first thing that you want to do is create something that I call dopamine debt. We all know this neurotransmitter that's associated with pleasure, with motivation, with actually achieving things. Dopamine debt is essentially where you try to eliminate all of the dopamine that you have accured throughout the day or even if it's first thing in the morning to essentially set your baseline level of dopamine so freaking low. so incredibly low that anything that you do afterwards is going to feel so much better than what you just did before. We are starved in this world of true boredom. Every second of the day, we are just thrown constant dopamineergic activities with social media, porn, with food, with relationships, every single freaking minute of the day. And dopamine, you know, typically es and flows. And if you are like doom scrolling on IG and your dopamine levels are continually getting spiked and spiked and come back down and spike and come back down and then you try to get into this super deep state of focus. No, of course that's not going to work because your brain is comparing this deep work that you're doing to this super dopamineergic activity of scrolling social media. It's not going to be a smooth transition. So instead what you're going to do is acrue dopamine debt which is literally you sit on your bed wherever where there is zero stimuli around you. What I mean by this is no phone, no music, nothing. Bonus points if you do this with no sunlight because sunlight, as many of you know, does increase your dopamine. However, that's better though. Allow sunlight. You know, I don't personally do sunlight, but I'll allow it. You're going to sit in silence. You're going to stare at a wall and do nothing for 10 minutes before you even trigger this state of deep work and true focus. Now, this dopamine debt that you're building up is essentially intentional boredom. And as I said, because of, you know, especially the invention of the smartphone, but everything else in modern society, everything is instant. And that doesn't really go well with the natural way that dopamine is supposed to be released in your brain. So, you're essentially building in boredom on purpose so that right before you get into your deep work states, it's not going to feel like you're swimming upstream compared to if you were like scrolling reels right before you get into your deep work. It's it's going to not be easy. You may stay focused for like 10 minutes and then you're going to lose because your attention span is reactive to whatever you were doing before after you have completed your dopamine debt. For example, the most the most common example is people that go to college and they have to write this big essay by the end of the year. It's really as simple as that. Next up on the list, we have the add toart to purchase ratio. This is the percentage of people, hence the name, that purchase out of the total add to carts. And the KPI here is 30%. So if I had 10 people add my product to cart, I want to see at least three go through and purchase. If it's below 30%, this tells you that there's a breakdown in between the time where somebody adds an item to cart and actually purchases, which is either the cart page or the checkout page. The one very simple way that most people can solve this issue is [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] please, for all that is holy, test your checkout. I can't tell you how many people I've seen, they haven't even seen what their checkout looks like and then they go through and see that like they have issues with their payment processor and the approval rates there and they can't [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] approve anybody because of that. Or I see that there's literally like technical issues that are keeping people from purchasing. You have no idea. Every time I launch a new funnel, it's part of our QC SOP that you have to buy from it before it's even sent to cold traffic. You have to test it yourself first because I promise you'll probably see stuff a little bit differently when you go through that entire process yourself. But in the case that this is not some sort of technical issue, a few other things that I see is that there's always these objections and concerns that people have when buying online. Maybe, for example, they live in an area where you don't know if there's free shipping. So add free worldwide shipping as a ban or simple solution there. Maybe they see that there is paid shipping and they're super, you know, adverse to that. It can always be different things, but again, just going through and testing it can give you a really good idea of why this is so low in the first place. But also remember, it only matters in the context of your conversion rate. If your conversion rate is good, your maybe your ad to car ratio is higher and this is a little bit lower, that's totally fine. As long as the CVR is good in general, you're chilling. Next up is your average order value. It's kind of in the [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] name. You know, it's the average amount that people pay for one order on your store. Now, my KPI here once again is very aggressive and a lot of people don't agree with it, but hey, you're here watching my video, not theirs. It should be a minimum of 2x or front-end price. Okay, what I mean by front-end price is what you're selling your main product for on the front end of your funnel. So, if I'm selling a $40 product, my KPI for AOV is now $80. And I know that may seem like a lot to a lot of you, but I promise you, if you can collect a lot of cash up front, we're we're playing the LTV game ultimately. There's only two ways you can win at LTV and that's either collecting more cash up front or more cash on rebuild. So for the majority of you, you're probably not ready for subscriptions and to go down that route. So playing the AOV maxing game is a good strategy. And the way that you can get this higher AOV is from a better offer and better structured upsells and other price points. So that's really all you can do in terms of AOV. But there's so many different ways to do that. There's so many different offers you can test. There's so many different upsells you can have. You can have pre-purchase, you can have incart, you can have post purchase, you can have Otto's. Like I could go down the list of all the different ways you can have digital product, physical product, you know, shipping, free shipping. It's because there's so much that you can do to get more cash from your customers. And this used to be something, by the way, that was optional. It used to be the case where you could be profitable just selling one product at like, you know, 2 to 3x rorows, 5x rorowes, and never worry about upsells, never worry about LTV. You're just printing. It's 2026 now, and that's not the case anymore. Okay? You have to do this if you want to be profitable. Next up is your LTV to AOV ratio. LTV stands for lifetime value. We already went over AOV, but lifetime value is how much money one single customer is going to pay you in the lifetime of them interacting with your business. My KPI here is a 3:1 ratio. So, if my AOV on the front end is, let's say, $40, then my goal LTV has to be at least 120, which means I'm going to have to sell the customer multiple times afterwards, most likely, you know, to get three times the amount as what I collected on the very first go. This number basically tells you how good are you at reselling your customers? What is your return purchase rate? Acquiring a customer is one of the most expensive things that you can do in your business. Once you have a customer, you can sell to them over and over and over again. But that is a skill in and of itself. And lastly on the list right now, we have your CAC, which is your cost to acquire a customer. Now, I put this on the funnel side, but truthfully, this is funnel and ad side. And this is one of the most important, if not the single most important metric in this entire video, because it tells you how much you have to pay to get an actual customer. Now, this of course depends a lot on your personal unit economics, your gross margins, but my KPI here is I want it to be at least less than 50% of my AOV. So, if my AOV to acquire one single customer is $100, I want my CAC to be well under 50. So, if you're looking at a rorowass perspective, that would be roughly a 2x row. Again, this depends a lot on your specific gross margins. So, keep that in mind, but this is a general good rule because that puts you at a 2x row. Now, your goal here, of course, is you want to have a small CAC. Okay? Regardless of what people told you about having big CAC, no, small CAC is king. Now, one of my favorite analogies is this hose analogy from Alex Ramos, which is if your business and you acquiring traffic and customers was a hose, somewhere in that hose, there's a bend. There's this constraint. And the reason that I have been making this video and talking about these sliders is that each one of those potentially could be kind of this creek in the hose. and it's your job to identify what the constraint is and then apply the right strategies to completely fix that constraint. Maybe it's your poor CTR, maybe it's your high CPMs, maybe you have a really poor add to cart ratio. But this framework tells you exactly number one what the constraint actually is and number two how to actually solve it so that you can have the water and traffic flow freely through your entire funnel so that you're as profitable as humanly possible. Now, just to go over a few common combinations that I see here, now that you know what all the metrics are, one of the most common I see is having really high CPMs, high CPC, and low CTR. What this most commonly means is you are marketing in a incredibly competitive market. So let's say your CTR in this case is like 6%. Remember our KPI was three. And what I mean by that is you are acquiring the worst of the worst people on your funnel in terms of the traffic and you're getting all these curiosity clickers, all these people that have no desire whatsoever to actually buy from you and you're just baiting the click using engagement tactics. This is basically like if content creators got into ecom, this would be their main [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] problem. Great at getting attention, [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] at converting it. The only other, you know, potential thing that this could be is your funnel is just absolute dog [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] So like maybe you're really good on the ad side, but even to be like this good is very very rare. It's actually just their unit economics and be able to extract as much cash as humanly possible on that first purchase. All right, and to end this video, I have two final important things to say. Like if you were viewing things in a vacuum in in this black box and you're like, "Okay, my ads are printing. I have a super low CPC. CPMs are phenomenal. CTR is phenomenal. My ads are cooking. It's the ability to make something stand out from the background, to be able to stand out from the noise. A lot of times in web development, this is used as well. And you can see a really good example of having a good contrast ratio would be something like having black text on a white background or having white text on a black background or something like this. Or maybe you've seen this warm paper. All of these have very high contrast. Now, on the other hand, you have an example of low contrast where the text here is clearly not standing out whatsoever. So every ad you create, every piece of content that you create, your job is to create the most amount of contrast in the mind of the customer. This is literally like the origin of everything because everybody's being bombarded by marketing material every single day. It's not about like creating good marketing. It's creating marketing material that is allowing you to stand out from everything else. And so if I'm going to go and enter a new market, this is literally what I'm thinking. I'll go look at the top competitors in the space. I'll go look at top content creators in the space. I'll look at what everybody is doing and I will ask one simple question which is how can I do the exact opposite? You know, who would have thought? And so, this rule is called no clean hands because your goal is to get your hands [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] dirty. If you're trying to be one of those entrepreneurs that's like, "Oh, I'm going to like hire this person to do everything for me. It's not a good use of my time. I should be focusing more leverage stuff. Well, I learned something from studying a lot of startups when I was younger. And one of the rules that I learned is in order to scale, you have to do things that don't scale, especially in the beginning. So, what that means is you are creating a lot of [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] ads yourself. You are writing a lot of [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] copy yourself. You are creating a lot of [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] content yourself. It's going to be manual. It's going to feel annoying. It's going to feel like there's better ways to do this. But this process is one of the best ways for you to self-learn and actually get really freakishly good. I would not be the marketer that I am now if I just tried to like outsource everything that required an ounce of effort. The reason that I'm so good is because I love being in the [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] trenches, man. I never want to get out of the dayto-day. I don't want to have a business that just runs autopilot. Okay, that sounds pretty good actually. But I don't want to do that anyways because I'm stubborn. And the real truth is if you want to get freakishly good at something, you have to do it a shitload of times, okay? And you have to start with something that doesn't scale if you want to scale. So get your hands dirty, start making [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] get inside the ad account, start posting a million pieces of content, whatever it is for you. Just get [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] active because that's how you get your reps in. That's how you get your 10,000 hours in and become an ultimate master at what you do. Rule number two is money loves speed. I am also completely psychotic about moving quickly. I've had a lot of people work for me. It doesn't always work out. And many of the times when I talk to people after they leave my company, I was like, why do you think we are where we are? You know, we're growing really quickly. What is it about us that's different from everybody else? And I get the same response every time, which is just you guys move freakishly fast. So, the point here is really to reduce the amount of time it takes for you to get data and get feedback. The faster and shorter and tighter that you can make your feedback loop, the faster you're going to see results and actually start making some money. So for example, if we have an idea for, you know, a new ad, we test it and it starts actually getting some results, we test the thing and we get data. If I see that that ad, that particular ad in this case is getting results. It's getting super profitable, low CPA, etc., I start making iterations of that ad halfway through the day. I don't like to just like throw stuff out and just like sit back and relax. No, if I see a few small positive indicators initially, as long as there's like significant data, we are immediately moving and iterating on that. And if you think this like sounds complex, we learned this [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] in freaking middle school, if not elementary school. This is literally just us applying the scientific method, but squeezing the time that it takes to get results and start to iterate to as small of a period of time as possible, right? We ask a question. We make a hypothesis. We start to construct a test. We experiment. We get conclusions. Then we immediately start over and start hypothesizing. Once again, it's just a scientific method. It's nothing new, right? You make an observation, hypothesis, you build it, test it, get the data, and immediately start to move and iterate on it. This is so underrated, guys. I can't even tell you because most people are slow as [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] They're so [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] slow. A lot of companies out there are corporate. They move at the speed of snails. Especially if you're younger, you have a massive, massive advantage when it comes to speed. Lean the [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] into it. Rule number three is to assume nothing. As entrepreneurs, a lot of times we think that we know what's going to convert better, that we know that this offer is going to do better, this ad looks better, therefore it's going to do better. All of those are assumptions. The only thing that is real is data. Anything before you get data is an assumption and should be left at the [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] door. Don't assume that your funnel is good. Don't assume your creatives are good. Don't just like test stuff because you like it. You don't know your customers as well as you probably think you do. You don't know what is going to convert nearly as well as what you probably think will. And anybody that has run ads for a long time knows this to be true. A lot of the [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] times the ads that end up being the most profitable, making the most amount of money. Guess what? They're [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] ugly. They look like [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] And eventually you kind of step back and you're like, I can't even believe that this ad has spent so much profitable ad spend over the last year. Like, it's just mindboggling. But data is data. Data does not lie to you. It always tells you the truth of what is happening in reality. As long as you're looking at the right data, of course. So assume nothing. Test all your assumptions. Like the only time that you can have something be true is if you test it and you have data to back it up. Rule number four, comparison is theft. Last but not least out of this how to think section, rule number five, you have to break the rule. So that's how to think. Now thinking doesn't get you any results. Thinking doesn't make you any money. Action does. So let's talk about section two, which is how to create. Now, I need to make a really important distinction here because some of you guys may not have run ads or, you know, be familiar with running ads. There's a very important distinction which is that copy is not the same as content. In fact, I would actually view them on opposite ends of the spectrum. If you think of a spectrum being like from direct response, you know, which would be like over here to brand, this is exactly how it look. Copy would be way over here on direct response. Content would be over here on brand. The purpose of content is to get engagement, to get likes, to get clicks, is to get views. It is not to get sales. Copy on the other hand, its sole job is to get sales. And they are not the same thing. My favorite thing to say about this is if you look at the people that are the best at getting the attention, are they the best at converting that attention?
