And that's why most content creators are broke because they have no idea to write copy, but they're good at getting views. So I guess that's freaking cool. And I think any copywriter, you know, you're like, "Okay, more copy is king. Like how do I write good copy?" This is not going to be a copywriting video. This is more principlebased. But the foundation of copy, as any good copywriter knows, is simply going to be your research. Your goal is to understand your customers better than they know themselves. Fortunately for you, AI has made this wickedly easy to get incredible deepsided research about your customers. It can be done in a span of like 30 minutes now with deep research tools and in a gent workflow, which I'll touch on a bit later. But your goal from the research is to extract as much gold as possible so that you can number one speak the language of your customer which is something called VOCC, voice of customer. A lot of people write copy for them. You're not writing copy for you. You're writing copy for your customer. Use their language. Use their phraseology. Speak the way that they would speak. I believe that copy is like learning a different language. It's very very foreign. oftentimes very counterintuitive if you've been raised to write, you know, the way that English class teaches you to write or college classes teach you to write. I would never hire a copywriter who like has an English degree or like a journalism degree. Jesus Christ, would that be a [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] mess. I remember before AI, all the best copywriters that I knew would always say like 80% of your time should be spent on research. The next like 5% is actually writing the copy, which is the easy part, and then the extra 15% is chiefing the copy. And chiefing is simply the process of reviewing your copy through the lens of your customer and also through the lens of a copywriter. Now, one of the most foundational things in all of copywriting. I already said one, which is the stages of awareness and the law of mass desire. Number two or three, whatever I'm on, is going to be the stages of awareness. Now, stages of awareness is every customer in your market is at a different stage. Some people are completely unaware, which would be this section right here where they're not even aware of the problem that they have. And then, of course, problem aware, solution aware, product aware, most aware. I don't think I have to explain it. This is relatively straightforward. But point being, when you're writing your copy, you want to be writing to a specific stage of awareness because as soon as you start mixing the stages, you're not talking to just one person. You're talking to multiple. And that's the whole crux of the issue with copywriting. Like you want to be writing your copy as if you're speaking directly to one very hyperspecific person. Now, you can take somebody from problem aware to solution aware to product aware, but you can't try to talk to somebody who is unaware or problem aware in the same way that you talk to somebody who is solution aware or product aware. It's just not going to work. But the deeper layer of this is, of course, you want to write to a specific stage of awareness, but you also want to write to a specific level of consciousness. And this is one of those things that I never really heard marketers really talk about at all until I decided to kind of link them myself. But once you start kind of diving into the whole quantum physics, metaphysics, you start to understand that there's layers deeper than just our own biology that we don't understand very well. But a small group of people have started to understand. And that would be something such as your map of consciousness. By the way, obviously developed by David Hawkins, amazing, amazing author. Go read all of his books. But Dr. David Hawkins was able to understand that every single person is calibrating at a specific level of consciousness. And your goal when it comes to writing copy, kind of like a three-step process. Number one is to always meet them where they are. So imagine if you know you're an English speaker and somebody comes up to you and just starts speaking a completely foreign language. You're going to have no idea what they're saying. Obviously, you're not going to get anything. It's like they're just speaking gibberish to you. Well, the same is true with meeting them at their level of awareness and meeting them at the specific level of consciousness. And it sound a little woo woo, but it should be resonating at a specific frequency that resonates with where the customer is at right now. So, for example, I believe that the best way to write really, really good copy is meet them where they're at, of course. But then second, like let's say you meet them at, you know, the level of fear, just as an example, right? Calibrating at 100 on the energetic log. As soon as you meet them there, your goal is to raise their consciousness. So, you don't want to just keep all of your copy in their current state because if you do that, then they're never going to feel the aspiration to get to a higher state of consciousness through solving their problem, through the use of your product. So your goal is to raise their level of consciousness into something like courage, neutrality, willingness, etc. Of course, you're never going to get to enlightenment. But you know, your goal is to raise the level so that they can feel what it would be like to actually have this particular problem solved. If you look at the level of fear as an example, think of the survival niche. What is kind of the core emotion that is being dominated there? I would say not the core emotion, the core level of consciousness that's being dominated is fear, right? People are afraid of something that's going to happen in the future. And so that's why you can meet them there. Bring them to a higher level. And then immediately you want to bring them back down. Now the reason you do this, and I know this is going to sound like kind of crazy, but this is legitimately how I write copy and how I think of advertising in general is if this is kind of their their level of energy, their level of consciousness, and this is time. What you want to do, of course, is, you know, meet them where they are, bring them up that high level, and then bring them back down to their original level. The reason that you bring them back down is simply because your goal ultimately is to create the highest what I call emotional delta. When you create a massive emotional gap in somebody and they feel that like they feel where they are, where they could have been, and then they're brought back down to where they are, that is when you can get people to take action and ultimately buy your product at an incredibly high rate. I think the best example of this is, you know, a lot of successful entrepreneurs, they got really, really rich and then they lost it all and then they got rich again. What hurt worse? Was it the time that they were initially broke, like for example over here, or was it the time that they were broke for the second time? I believe that it was the time that they were broke for the second time. The reason why is because they now know what it feels like to be rich. Initially, they didn't. They were just broke. But once you know how good it can feel to have your problem solved and you're still brought back down to the base level, that's when truly incredible things can happen in your copy and your advertising. And so, this should all be extracted from the research that you do. Now, the final piece on copy and research that I want to say is research obviously, if you haven't gotten it by now, it's pretty freaking important. But most of the research that people do is on these Reddit forums, it's on Facebook groups, it's on, you know, Tik Tok, it's on Instagram. But what about the best source of research that you could ever have? Your best source of research is the words directly from your own customers. And the reality is, unfortunately, the large majority of entrepreneurs, especially in the e-commerce space, they'll never speak with their customers. You have your customers. Speak to them. That's kind of like the beauty of being an entrepreneur, too. Like, you're creating this thing. You're helping people, you know, improve their lives. Why would you not speak to them? You'll be able to create better products, but you also be able to create better marketing. This is a true story. And I took that very very specific phrase word for word I used it as a hook on one of my ads and it ended up becoming one of the most profitable ads in my entire ad account. That is the value of getting customer research. This isn't like public forum stuff. No, speak to your customers. you'll get some of the best research, some of the best hook ideas, some of the best inspiration you could ever have. Now, speaking of hooks, let's talk about hooks. Okay, we all know that the first of anything is the most important in marketing. If you're writing a video ad, the first 3 to 5 seconds is the most important, right? What we call the hook. If you're writing an email, it's going to be the subject line. If you're writing a sales letter, it's going to be the headline. Or if you're making regular content, it's going to be the hook as well. And ultimately, this is where you should be spending 90% of your time. like literally 90% of your time because if you don't get people hooked in initially, you're never going to have that. And so there's a good framework by legendary copywriter Chris Adod. Shout out to this dude. He had a framework within copy called the pig framework, which is punch in the gut. And he was using it specifically in the eyes of opening a VSSL with the lead or with an ad. But I think this is actually pretty true in marketing in general. A lot of times you do want to come with that first sentence, that first piece of copy and your goal is to make it [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] punch. Like I would be reviewing, you know, some of my copywriters work back in the day. I don't really have copywriters anymore because of AI, but when I was, the piece of feedback that I would always give is like, we just have to make this punch harder. Like that was like word for word what I repeat time and time again like we have to make this punch harder. And it would always be like the subject line or the hook or the headline. It has to be so vivid, so visceral, so emotional that it almost just feels like a punch in the gut, either in a good way or a bad way. And one of the biggest mistakes I see people make when it comes to, we'll just call it the hook, but it's like the first of anything. They try to be clever. They try to impress people with their copy. And one of my greatest copy might writing mentors that I've had in my journey, she would always repeat the same thing, which is that you always want to prioritize clarity over cleverness. Clarity is going to win every single day of the week. In fact, that's literally a step in my copy chiefing process is to make sure everything is crystal [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] clear. And a lot of times that can be as simple as bringing your copy down to a fifth grade reading level or if you want to be even a little bit extra, you know, an eighth grade reading level. I probably would not ever go beyond an eighth grade reading level. But that's a step in the process because I mean most people are not able to understand copy that's written at a higher level than eighth grade or not even that they're able to understand it. Maybe they can, but it's not going to resonate with them as deeply. You want to speak clearly. You want to clarify your thoughts, your copy as clearly as possible. Not try to impress them with these super cool copywriting tactics and techniques. A lot of times copy that is doesn't sound super impressive, but it's just so clear and so direct converts better than the super duper indirect clever direct response on steroids kind of copy. And I want to take a second to talk specifically about, you know, the hook of a video because I believe that the hook is actually kind of made up of three total parts. You have the copy of the hook which is the first like one or two sentences that you're writing. You have the audio and then you also have of course the video which we'll call the scroll stopper. So the beautiful part about this is the hook of an ad. Like if this entire thing is a hook, there's actually three different ingredients that you have to leverage. If I had to give them weight, I would say that roughly the copy is going to be about 80%. The next 15% is going to be the scroll stopper and the final 5% will be the audio. Maybe the audio is like a voice over. Maybe it's music, maybe it's AI voiceover, real voice over. You get the idea. But you should be using all these together. You know, one of the frameworks that I use for scroll stoppers is to find the clips that you would watch and you would have to verbally say out loud, "What the [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] did I just watch?" Like that's the emotion. That's the phrase that you're trying to get somebody to say if you saw the hook. Like if you went up to your mom and you have your school stop on your phone, you were like, "Mom, check this out." And she watches it and she's like, "What the [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] was that?" You're like, "Perfect." and then you go throw it in your ads manager because that's going to be the best performing scroll stopper that you have. That is what you want out of a really really good scroll stopper. Now the copy of course I already kind of touched on and the audio you know simple voice over typically does the trick. If you want to be a little extra, you know, go with AI, you know, it's ultimately up to you, but it's the smallest needle mover, so we don't really need to talk about it too much. So you kind of know how hooks work. You understand all the ingredients. Now, where do you like get inspiration from? I want to take a second to actually talk about swiping. If you don't know what swiping is, it's simply the act of looking at copy that is already performing and basically rewriting it for your specific niche or market or whatever. This is super duper important. Okay? Do not swipe blindly. Do not swipe blindly. What I mean by that is I very very rarely will swipe ads like one to one. When I'm swiping ads, I'm not taking the copy. What I'm doing is I'm actually swiping the philosophy. All [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] aside. I think this is one of the the reasons that I've been able to be pretty good and and successful with writing ads and writing copy in general because if you swipe, you never [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] learn anything, like you're just taking something that was already working. You change a few words, like boom, you're good. Don't get me wrong, it can work a little bit. But what you should really be doing to learn how to run really, really good ads and you're like, you're in this for the long term. You're like, I want to be a phenomenal marketer. I just want to be an absolute savage. Like, I could write anything and make money. If that's you, swipe philosophy. And what I mean by that, when you're looking at an ad, what you're really trying to break it down to is the frameworks of why it was successful, not the copy itself. Because if you swipe the philosophy, you can extract a framework that you can apply to anything. You can apply that to any ad, any niche. It basically just goes in your massive like memory bank that's supposed to be a brain. Uh it goes into your brain and massive memory bank of things that can help you write really really good ads in the future. And if you're really a G, you can throw all of those philosophies and all of those frameworks into AI and you'll just be able to create like millions of different variations that are all really good without having to rely on just like directly copying onetoone what somebody else is doing. So if you can understand the underlying psychological principles that are really at play, you basically develop principles first thinking, which means you can apply it to anything. If you just swipe, it's like basically like the equivalent of like building a pump and dump drop shipping store. like you make a little bit of profit and then you end up losing most of it and then you end up chasing the next shiny object versus building a brand where you have something long-term that pays you every single month. It's much more consistent. You're not reliant on like hopping trends. If you swipe, it's going to work until it doesn't. Then what? Then you have to move on and try to swipe the next thing and again and again and again. Learn how to think, okay? Build your own mental pipeline of philosophies that you've extracted from ads because you'll get better at diagnosing your own, but you'll also just write better copy long term. Next, let's say you got your copy, you got your creatives made. I want to talk about how you can maximize your chance of success through removing unknown variables. So, anytime that you launch something new, whether that's a new business, new product, new ad, whatever, there's all these unknowns. There's all these variables that are affecting how profitable that ad is going to be. Among them, you have something like the media buying. Basically, how are you actually launching your ads? Yes, that does affect how profitable it's going to be. You have the creative itself, which is going to be, you know, the biggest variable of all and ironically the one that you can't change. You have the copy associated with that creative. You have the product that you're actually selling. You have the entire market. You have the Facebook algorithm. You know, maybe it's just a poor day on the algo and everybody is experiencing it and that could be the reason that an ad ends up not being successful. This is a small list of six things that I have on my screen right now, guys. I could probably jot down like 30 or 40 different variables. The reason why I'm talking about this is because you want to remove everything that's potentially in your way of getting results and minimize the variables that are going to be affecting your success. The one that I see most commonly violated is people will try to launch products with a product that is not even proven to sell. Like especially in early stage ecom, this was super duper big. You know, finding the unicorn winning product. Nobody else has sold it before. You're the first one. Soon as you start pushing traffic to it, you are printing. Well, the only problem with that is amongst all the other variables, your ads, your media buying, your copy, your hook, your scroll stopper, like everything that goes into that ad actually being successful, now you added an additional one, which you don't know if anybody actually wants your [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] product. All right? So, after all that work that you just did, you decided to sell something that wasn't proven. Why? There's already so many other things you have to worry about. Again, this is a non-exhaustive list. There's so many things that can be added here and you're going to add more variables like testing something that isn't proven really. I'll tell you right now, the most successful marketers that I know, they're like fening for proven markets. They want to go into where people are already buying [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] And if you try to be this unicorn selling a product that's never been sold before, maybe you make it, you know, maybe you end up being massively successful and you can come back and slap me in the face if you want to. But for the majority of people, there's no need. There's no reason. There's already so much [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] you have to deal with. Don't add additional variables. Remove them all. Remove every unknown variable that's affecting your success. Okay? Success is not a game of addition. It's a game of subtraction. Remove all the [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] and the success will find you. Now, the last piece until we move on to the economics is going to be the concept of ugly ads equals pretty profits. Aesthetics in the eyes of marketing is incredibly [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] overrated, especially when it comes to paid advertising. What looks best most often is not what converts. And that's another reason why most content creators are broke is because they try to make their ads look like [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] cinematic masterpieces. And you know what? Every once in a while, they do actually work. But as I said previously, the most successful ads that I have ever written in my entire life look like [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] These are the ones that spend the most that are the most profitable simply because they're clear. Like it's not that like there's a direct proportion between like how ugly an ad is and how much money that ad is going to make you. No, it's more so stop optimizing for aesthetic. Start optimizing for clarity. As I said before, that's really the secret behind behind these ugly ads. Maybe they don't look great, but god damn is the copy good, but god damn, is it [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] clear what they're trying to get across. All right, moving on to one of my favorite pieces. Part three is going to be all about economics. Now, I've actually never heard the economics of a business really explained in this way, but I actually think economics can be broken down into three main sectors. First, you have the economics of your ads. I'm going to be using paid advertising mostly as an example here by the way. You have the economics of the ads themselves. You have the economics of your funnel. And lastly, you have the economics of your backend, which for simplicity purposes will basically say everything that happens after you acquire that first customer, that first customer purchase. So, let's first talk about the economics of your ads. This is going to be all of your soft metrics, right? This is going to be your ad spend. This is going to be your CPMs, which is your cost to reach a,000 impressions. This is going to be your CPC. This is going to be your CTR. It's going to be all the soft metrics that you want to add on here. Now, your goal, if I had to put it into one sentence, is to get the lowest cost of traffic, the chief of traffic cost that you possibly can with the highest quality. All right? I can't just say you want to have the cheapest traffic known to man because if you do, you're just going to start running ads in India and see that you have 50 cent CPMs and your CPC doesn't even register. It's below 1 cent and nobody [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] buys from you. So, it's not only about how inexpensive the traffic is, it's also about how qualified that traffic is for your offer. I don't care if you're doing ecom, affiliate, high ticket, this is true regardless. And then you have the economics of your funnel. And your goal here ultimately is once again a two-sided coin. Your goal is to get the highest number of conversions, which we'll just say is sales in this case, with the highest average order value per sale. Now, this is kind of where it gets into debatable territory cuz some people say that it's not good to have a high AOV and you should instead rely on LTV play of multiple, you know, rebuilds and purchases on the back end. But me personally, I think you can have both. I think that you can have high AOV front end and still have really solid repeat purchase rate and LTV. But long story short, the goal of your funnel is to convert as many people as humanly possible at the highest amount and extract as much cash as possible that you can from each customer that comes through, which is your AOV, your average order value. There's another legendary marketer I want to shout out, Dan Kennedy, who said, "The guy that can spend the most to acquire a customer wins." And I'll tell you something, it's a lot easier to be able to spend more on ads if you collect more money from every customer than everybody else. Cuz that means you have more cash, more profit at the end of the day to reinvest back into your ad. That means that you can scale faster. You can scale with better margins. Because look, I'll give you a really quick example. Let's say that me and my competitor both are selling the same product, right? This is me and this is my competitor. We're both paying $50 to acquire a customer, right? That is our TAC, right? Our customer acquisition cost. The only difference is every customer that he buys is worth, let's say, $80. And every customer that I buy, I'll be very conservative here, is worth $100. The only difference here is a measily $20 in margin, right? Doesn't really seem like a whole lot. But what this really means is I can spend an additional $20 to acquire a customer. And so in the eyes of Facebook, in the eyes of, you know, advertising algorithms, if I have an additional $20 to spend, what that really means is instead of having to spend $50, I can now spend up to $70 to acquire a customer, whereas my competitor can only spend 50. So I can outbid my competitors because I can afford to spend more to acquire a single customer. Because acquiring a customer is a hard part. Once you have a customer, you can resell them. You have their email, you have their phone number, you have their data. You can do whatever you want with this customer at this point. Like the hard work is done. The customer has been acquired. And as I said, the business that can spend the most to acquire a customer is the one that will win. So if you maximize your average order value, that by definition means as long as your margins are in place that you can spend more to acquire each additional consecutive customer substantially more than your competitors, you're going to destroy your competition because you can afford more to acquire customers. So that is your funnel economics. And then lastly, we have your backend economics, which as I said is essentially everything that happens after that first initial visit through the funnel is complete. This is where you see your repeat purchase rate. This is where a lot of people build subscriptions into their business. They build MR, ARR, all that good stuff. The goal here is very, very simple, which instead of having two variables, there's only one. Your goal is to maximize your LTV, right? Your customer lifetime value. That is the total amount of cash that you collect from each customer on average. So, somebody buys a $100 product from you once, that means your AOV is 100, but your LTV is also 100, but maybe in, you know, two or three months, your customer comes back and buys that same $100 product once again. Now, the LTV is 200, but your AOV is still at 100. So, just so you understand what LTV actually is, that pretty simple, but that's what it is. LTV is where the real money is. If you look at any business that has been able to scale very successfully, very profitably, even go public, it's simply because they have mastered LTV. They've mastered retention. They've mastered the repeat purchase rate. If we look in the eyes of the low ticket world, which would be like ecom, low ticket info, you know, low ticket SAS as an example, the play there is typically subscription. So, acquiring a customer not just has a onetime purchase, but having them rebuild every 30, 60, 90 days. Supplement companies most recently have switched to this model. Like if you go look at a big supplement company and you go to their actual website, like not on Amazon, you go to their website, most of them are going to try to be forcing you into a subscription because they know their numbers, they know their economics, and they want to have customers coming back from them and buying repeatedly because they know customer acquisition costs are only going to increase. Like a lot of people think that when you start to scale a business, start to scale up your ad spend, that everything gets easier, everything gets worse. your customer acquisition cost goes up, you have to inject more creatives into the account, it becomes more difficult or maybe not more difficult, there just the challenges become different is probably a better way to put it. Now, on the other hand, if you're in like a high ticket business, for example, the way that you would build your LTV is typically through current, you know, high ticket subscriptions, it's a little bit more rare, but often times it's through ascensions. If you have if you have like a 5K program that you're selling and as soon as people, you know, finish like a 3-month 5K program, then they're going to be upsold into a 10K program, right? That's how a lot of high ticket businesses are going to be functioning there. And so, if you piece all of this together, what is really the full goal with all of this with the entire economics playbook? Well, if we piece them together one by one, the first thing that you want to do on the ad side, of course, is have cheap qualified traffic. Then once you push them through your funnel, of course you want to have max conversions that you possibly can at the highest average order value that you possibly can. And then of course number three is going to be the backend which is going to be increasing your LTV through subscription, through ascension, whatever is applicable to you. Now if we zoom out and we put all these pieces together, what you're really aiming to do is have a really high ratio of LTV to CAC, right? cuz that's how much money you're collecting from each customer in the history of your business compared to how much you're spending to acquire said customer. This is like one of the most basic baseline measurements of how healthy your business really is. And then don't get me wrong, this depends a lot on your business. Like high ticket has completely different KPIs from like ecom or affiliate or anything like that. So I would say as a bare minimum you want to be looking at a 3:1 ratio. If you're running high ticket, I would shoot closer for like a 7 to10 10:1 ratio LTV to CAC. But that means that if I have an LTV on average of $300, that I shouldn't be spending more than $100 to acquire each customer. Right? Pretty straightforward. Now, if you can't tell this far into this section, data is king. It is incredibly incredibly important to know your numbers like the back of your hand. All right? ===== 20260327_the_antidote_to_mediocrity [SDo2mMM4XYo] =====

Hey guys, Mark here. Most people are mediocre. They are average. They're ordinary. You clicked on this video because you don't want to end up like them. In fact, the very thought of being average just repulses you to your core, and that's why you're here. The bad news is, statistically speaking, most people aren't going to change. They're going to stay average. They're going to stay mediocre for their entire life. The good news is, there's a sneaky little trick that you can use that ensures that you will never be mediocre for the rest of your life. And the antidote to mediocrity is simply to be [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] Now, when I say being [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] you immediately start to think of all these preconceived notions and meanings associated with the word, but that's not the way that I use this word. The way that I define being [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] is three things. Number one, people who are [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] have an obsession. They have something in their life that they are genuinely obsessed about. I always say that when you're obsessed with something, success becomes a byproduct. And obsession isn't just dedication. It's not just like working hard. Obsession is a natural, genuine intellectual curiosity about something where you seriously want to spend every waking moment that you have in your life learning more about this thing, pouring your heart, mind, and soul into understanding this thing. Like if you were to be obsessed about the guitar, you're not just going to learn a few scales, or you're not just going to learn a few changes, a few chords. You're going to learn everything top to bottom. You're going to learn every genre, every song type, every way that you can possibly play, simply because you have a genuine desire to do so. And when you are obsessed, the cool part is you don't have to rely on willpower. You don't have to rely on discipline. You don't have to rely on staying up until 3:00 a.m. watching motivational videos just so that you can feel something and get a little boost of dopamine and motivation to do 20 push-ups at 3:00 in the morning when you decide you want to change your life. No, this is not that kind motivation. This is not that kind of video. Being obsessed is a one-way street. And once you go down it, you can't go back. And it is one of those things where if you're able to cultivate it, because it's not something that you just have or you don't. If you're able to cultivate it and grow it, it can be one of the greatest superpowers to not being average. In fact, being obsessed, by definition, means that you are not average, so you are not mediocre. The second piece of being [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] is the general lack of social optics. Humans are incredibly social creatures. You've probably heard the saying you're the average of five people that you spend the most time with. But if you look at the five people around you, are you genuinely happy with what you see? Most people would say no, and that's why your life is in the position that it's at. But what most people don't tell you is that you don't have to live life that way. And the people that are [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] and ultimately are able to be incredibly successful in whatever field they're pursuing, they understand that the way that other people think about them, the way that other people view them, beliefs about other people around the world, they do not let all those things sway them. ===== 20260421_i_tried_staring_at_a_wall_everyday_for_30_days_better_than_adderall [EX_5KEdQkWA] =====

Hey guys, Mark here. So, I tried staring at a wall every single day for 30 days to see what would happen to my focus. So, every day before I would enter my deep work zone where I need to do my most important disciplined work of the day, I would get in my chair, aim it directly at a wall. I wouldn't have my phone. I wouldn't have any food. I would barely have any sunlight in the room. It's just me and a wall. But after completing this challenge, I can say that this is probably one of the highest leverage things that you can do to improve your focus. But first, let me answer the question why I actually did this. because obviously it's to improve my focus. But why stare at a wall in the first place? Well, the reality is most of the videos out there on YouTube right now fall into a category of what I like to call dopamine slop. And it's basically a bunch of people that are very uneducated about how dopamine actually works in your brain where they tell you to do things like do a dopamine detox, which in reality is not even a real thing. Even the guy that invented the term dopamine detox said that it was never meant to be used. Literally, it's physically impossible to detox a neurotransmitter from your brain. So, most videos that I saw was either number one repackaged dopamine slop or just some kind of crazy hype video with David Gogggins on there telling you how you're not working hard enough and it's all a willpower issue. But that's the thing, most people don't actually have a discipline problem. What you actually probably have is a stimulation problem. And let me explain what I mean. So, your brain is really good at learning just about anything. If you repeatedly do an activity, your brain is naturally going to get good at it through a process called mileelination, which is where, as you can see here, when neurons are connecting, there are myelin sheets that are wrapping around them in order for the signal to travel faster, which just a fancy way of saying the more that you train your brain to exercise a specific stimuli, the better it's going to get at exercising that process. And again, there isn't really a problem inherently with the process of myelination in and of itself. The issue is what you are training your brain on. Now the core mechanism surrounding why I came up with this idea in the first place is I knew that your brain is one big comparison machine. It doesn't necessarily assign objective value to anything. Everything is viewed in comparison to one another. For example, if the only stimulation that I ever gave my brain was doing deep work, going outside, and exercising, to my brain, that's all that it would know. In the same way that if all I did was scroll TikTok, order Door Dash, and play video games all day, that's all my brain would know then. Because the problem is with most of you, the way that you work is before you go do your most important work, let's say you're studying, you know, for an exam and you have a bunch of stuff to do. If you're scrolling your phone and you have, you know, dopamine hit after dopamine hit after dopamine hit, that's the state that your brain is currently in. It doesn't have much to compare, at least in the short term, right before you get into your work. And so your actual deep work that you need to get done is so incredibly boring, so under stimulating. That's why it feels so challenging for you number one to get started and number two for you to actually complete the work you said you were going to do. So, as I said in the intro, most dopamine content is completely misunderstood or at the very least misrepresented. So, the dopamineergic neurons in your brain have two very distinct uses and this is what most people don't really talk about. The first use is what's called phasic dopamine. This is the spike. This is your scrolling Tik Tok. This is your watching porn. This is your doing hard drugs. This is really what spikes your dopamine where most of the issues come from when it comes to hijacking your focus. It's fast, sharp, and it's typically tied to novelty. Like every time you're scrolling Instagram reels, every reel is new. Very rarely do you see the same two pieces of content within the same period of 5 to 10 minutes. And that's actually why social media is so addicting. It's because these unpredictable rewards are actually even more stimulating than something that is predictable, like the dopamine release that you get when you eat a highly satiating food. The other use is called tonic dopamine. The exact opposite of phasic. Tonic dopamine is essentially your motivational floor. It's your brain's baseline readiness to expend energy towards a specific goal. It's not the spike. It's a setting. It's a baseline. Now, you can raise and lower that baseline, but nonetheless, it is a baseline. Now, to explain this concept a little bit more, let me show you what, for example, scrolling Tik Tok doom scrolling does to these two types of activity when it comes to dopamine. So up on the top here we have your phasic activity as you can see aggressive spike and aggressive comedown and your tonic floor is going to be decreasing over time. You don't want your tonic floor to decrease. You actually want to see this steady if not increasing. The way to think about your tonic level is essentially your dopamineergic battery. So your phasic activity is like a notification that turns on your phone and just slightly drains the battery each time that you do it. And if you're just constantly lighting up your screen, of course, naturally, as you can see, your battery is going to go down. In fact, if we add in some more, you know, highly phasic dopamine spikes here, you can see just how much it's decreasing over time to the point where it's actually below the threshold that you need to make your deep work actually feel stimulating. And then you get to a point like this where your batter is basically empty. And it feels so damn difficult for you just to get started on something that it's actually somewhat difficult for your brain to execute. And to be clear, the solution here is not a dopamine detox. We all know the people that detox anything typically just end up on the exact same path that they were before. I ultimately started to stare at a wall every single day to fix this problem because I knew that it wasn't going to be something that I just like cold turkey stop everything cuz I know I'm just going to relapse back into that, you know, the same activities that I was beforehand. But if we look at something like staring at a wall, you can see just how drastically different it is. Both on the phasic activity, it's very steady and your tonic floor is incredibly high, which means you always have a high battery, which means actually doing your most important work feels easy. Not only that, but after you complete it, you genuinely feel good about yourself. You don't feel that like drudgy, you know, anti-motivational feeling where you just don't want to do anything and you kind of end up assigning a bunch of guilt to yourself and feel like a lazy piece of None of that happens when you make this change. And here's the few things that actually happened. Number one, it replaced all those super crazy spike phasic dopamine activities with tonic dopamine activity. So just imagine your brain is kind of like a backlog. I literally consider, you know, my brain sometimes almost like an LLM, like an AI tool. The most recent stuff is going to be your most well-remembered information. Like it's much harder to remember something from 10 years ago than it was 5 minutes ago, of course. And so I want to make sure that whatever comes right before my most important work should not be super high phasic activity. It should be tonic dopamineergic activity which hence is the reason that I stared at a wall. Now number two, you actually get some really cool benefits outside of just being able to make work feel easier. For example, when you are doing something like staring at a wall, you actually activate an area in your brain called the DMN, which stands for the default mode network. So this is a network in your brain that activates when external stimulation drop. When you stop working, your brain just doesn't magically turn off. It's still working. It's just working in a different way. And there's typically lower levels of stimulation. But this is the time where your brain does really cool kind of like behindthe-scenes activity like internal thought, self-reflection, maybe like some detached problem solving. It's like if you ever spend time in solidarity, like no phone, no music, no stimulation. Do you ever realize that you just kind of start to reflect on your life and like question all your choices? That is the DMN in effect. So, here's what happens when you actually do stare at a wall right before you do deep work for 30 days straight. Now, let me make something really clear. The goal here with staring at a wall is not to get into some super meditative state. Meditation is great. I think most people on the planet should be doing it. It's overall an incredible net positive. But the goal here, just to be clear, is to become less dependent on stimulation to begin something that's difficult. The actual problem for most people is that the gap between their stimulation baseline and what real work actually provides has gotten so wide that starting genuinely feels difficult. Now, here's how you can actually do this yourself to get the maximum amount of results and don't end up staring at a wall for no freaking reason. First, I want you to commit to a minimum of 30 days. All right? Dopamine research shows that typically to make meaningful changes, you'll start seeing changes after the first week, but really it'll take a few weeks to reset your system, if that makes sense. and more importantly reset your contrast of what is actually stimulating. Next 20 minutes a day, don't do it first thing in the morning unless you literally wake up and get immediately to work. You want to do it right before your most important deep work session, which leads into rule number three, which is make sure it's a high internal processing task. So whether that's writing, studying, problem solving, whatever time that you spend actually like creating and using your brain actively is when you should use this strategy. Now, just to set your expectations, this is going to suck at first. It's going to feel incredibly boring. It's going to be incredibly uncomfortable, but discomfort is the signal that this is actually working. All right, we all know focus is a game of subtraction, not addition. So, what we're doing is subtracting unnecessary stimulation so that real work feels incredibly stimulating. Now, the final piece here, it's not necessarily related to staring at a wall, but it's very, very important if you want this to work continuously. Most people after they complete their, you know, their work in general, what do they do? They get up from their desk or they walk out of the room and they go right on their phone. That ruins a lot of the work that you just did, a lot of the hard work, right? This is what I call a fake break. Fake breaks are not rest. It's just stimulation in another form. Like scrolling is not rest. Texting is not rest. YouTube is not rest. Those are just new forms of input, new forms of stimulation. And your brain is still processing. It's still reacting. It's still running the Q response loop. The only thing that changed is the content. So, in order to fully complete the loop and actually make this like a habit that sticks and make your next deep work session even easier, you have to close that habit loop. Not by getting on your phone, but by doing something that releases dopamine slowly and over time. For example, going on a walk, eating healthy, like doing something that's not going to immediately spike your dopamine. I think you know what is and isn't going to be doing that for the most part. And anybody who has children or pets knows that one of the worst things in the world is seeing your pet in physical pain. Like you would much rather have yourself go through that physical pain compared to your pet. And for that reason, it's also a very high pain market.
